meta.generated_by: hand-written seed set + questions adapted from past exams (SS21-SS22, mock) + questions grounded in the lecture notes + questions grounded in the programming exercises 0-4

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a common technique to tackle class imbalance?

options.A: Data augmentation
options.B: Batch normalization
options.C: Oversampling the minority class
options.D: Weighting the loss with the inverse class frequency

questions.explanation: Batch normalization normalizes activations; it does nothing about imbalanced label distributions.

questions.question: What is ensembling?

options.A: Multiple networks trained on the same task whose predictions are combined, e.g. by majority vote
options.B: Occluding different areas of the input image for visualization
options.C: Parallel paths with different kernel sizes inside one network
options.D: A network with multiple output heads for different tasks

questions.explanation: An ensemble combines several independently trained models to reduce variance.

questions.question: You build a food-image classifier. Which of these dataset situations is NOT a confound?

options.A: Spanish audio was recorded with a different microphone than Italian audio
options.B: Photos were taken with two different cameras, assigned randomly regardless of label
options.C: Car images were shot in daylight while pedestrian images were shot in rain
options.D: All T-shirt photos show women while all hoodie photos show men

questions.explanation: A nuisance factor is only a confound when it correlates with the label; random camera assignment does not.

questions.question: You have a model pre-trained on a similar task. What is the standard way to reuse it for your new classification task?

options.A: Retrain only the input layer
options.B: Freeze the feature-extraction layers and retrain the classification layers at the end
options.C: Retrain the feature extractor and keep the original classifier
options.D: Pick random layers to form a new model

questions.explanation: Transfer learning typically keeps learned features and retrains the task-specific head.

questions.question: A dataset contains 80% dog images and 20% cat images. A model reaches 80% accuracy. Why is this not impressive?

options.A: Accuracy cannot be computed on imbalanced data
options.B: 80% is below the theoretical minimum
options.C: The classes must first be normalized
options.D: A model that always predicts 'dog' already achieves 80%

questions.explanation: With imbalance, the majority-class baseline sets the bar; 80% equals always predicting dog.

questions.question: A mask/no-mask classifier reaches 98.1% accuracy, but the test data contains 981 'mask' and 19 'no mask' samples. What is the main doubt?

options.A: 98.1% is impossible
options.B: The learning rate was too high
options.C: The confusion matrix must be symmetric
options.D: The classes are highly imbalanced, so accuracy is misleading

questions.explanation: With 98% majority class, near-98% accuracy can be achieved while failing the minority class; use balancing or loss weighting.

questions.question: Which is a suitable way to evaluate the FINAL performance of a fine-tuned network?

options.A: Accuracy on the training set
options.B: Mean precision on a mix of training, validation and test data
options.C: Receiver operating characteristics curve on the test set
options.D: Recall on the training set

questions.explanation: Final evaluation must use held-out test data; training-set metrics measure fit, not generalization.

questions.question: What is the name of the method that re-uses pre-trained weights for a related task?

options.A: Transfer learning
options.B: Bagging
options.C: Self-distillation
options.D: Curriculum learning

questions.explanation: Reusing weights trained on one task as initialization for another is transfer learning.

questions.question: Which statement about input data normalization is true?

options.A: It is unnecessary because networks adapt during training
options.B: A common strategy is normalizing the data to zero mean and unit variance
options.C: It should be computed per sample independently
options.D: Test data should be normalized with statistics computed on the test set

questions.explanation: Standardization uses training-set statistics and is applied identically to validation/test data.

questions.question: Which of the following help to reduce overfitting? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Data augmentation
options.B: Dropout layers
options.C: Training for more epochs
options.D: Switching from SGD to full-batch gradient descent

questions.explanation: Augmentation and dropout regularize; batch mode and longer training do not reduce overfitting.

questions.question: Why does initialization matter for optimizing deep networks?

options.A: To reduce the number of parameters
options.B: Because the problem is convex
options.C: So training always reaches the same minimum
options.D: Because the optimization problem is generally non-convex

questions.explanation: In a non-convex landscape, the starting point influences which minimum is reached and how fast.

questions.question: Which statement about weight/bias initialization is TRUE?

options.A: It is important to calibrate the variance of the weights
options.B: Initializing the bias with 0 fixes the dying ReLU problem
options.C: Initialization matters because the problem is convex
options.D: If the bias is 0, its gradient is always 0 too

questions.explanation: Schemes like Xavier/He scale the weight variance to the fan-in so activations neither vanish nor explode.

questions.question: Which statement about initialization is FALSE?

options.A: Xavier initialization takes the number of input features into account
options.B: A small positive bias can help against dying ReLUs
options.C: With all-zero weights, the gradient w.r.t. the weights is zero
options.D: Initialization is important because deep learning problems are convex

questions.explanation: The problems are non-convex; that is exactly why initialization matters.

questions.question: What is the correct order to train a network with backpropagation and SGD? (D: init weights, C: forward pass, A: compute error, E: update weights, B: iterate)

options.A: D → A → C → E → B
options.B: D → B → C → A → E
options.C: D → E → A → B → C
options.D: D → C → A → E → B

questions.explanation: Initialize, forward, measure error, update, and repeat until converged.

questions.question: All weights AND biases are initialized to zero. Hidden activations are ReLU, the final activation is a sigmoid. For input x=(1,1,1,1), the output is:
questions.explanation: Every pre-activation is 0, ReLU(0)=0, and the final sigmoid(0)=0.5.

questions.question: An unknown final activation produced the output -0.0001. Which function could it be?

options.A: Softmax
options.B: ReLU
options.C: Leaky ReLU
options.D: Sigmoid

questions.explanation: ReLU, sigmoid and softmax never output negative values; leaky ReLU (and tanh) can.

questions.question: Which function does NOT fulfill the requirements for a useful activation function in deep learning?

options.A: 2x
options.B: tanh(x)
options.C: max(x, 0)

questions.explanation: 2x is linear, so stacking layers with it collapses to a single linear map.

questions.question: Why is the signum function not used as an activation in deep learning?

options.A: It is too expensive to compute
options.B: It does not introduce a non-linearity
options.C: Its derivative is either 0 or undefined for all inputs
options.D: It cannot output negative values

questions.explanation: With zero/undefined derivative everywhere, no gradient can flow, so gradient-based training fails.

questions.question: Which function models the all-or-nothing response of biological neurons?

options.A: The exponential
options.B: The logarithm
options.C: The threshold (step) function
options.D: The identity

questions.explanation: A threshold unit fires fully once the input exceeds a level and stays silent otherwise.

questions.question: Can the XOR problem be solved by a single-layer perceptron?

options.A: No — at least one hidden layer is needed
options.B: No — at least two hidden layers are needed
options.C: Yes
options.D: Only with support vector machines

questions.explanation: XOR is not linearly separable; one hidden layer with a non-linearity suffices.

questions.question: Two fully connected layers with NO activation in between (ŷ = σ(W₂(W₁x + b₁) + b₂)) are equivalent to a single layer ŷ = σ(W̃x + b̃) with:

options.A: W̃ = W₂W₁, b̃ = W₂b₁ + b₂
options.B: W̃ = W₂ + W₁, b̃ = W₂b₁
options.C: W̃ = W₁W₂, b̃ = b₁ + b₂
options.D: No such equivalence exists

questions.explanation: Multiplying out gives W₂W₁x + W₂b₁ + b₂ — without non-linearities depth adds nothing.

questions.question: Your model gives very different predictions across training runs, yet the average performance is high. The model has:

options.A: High bias, high variance
options.B: High bias, low variance
options.C: Low bias, high variance
options.D: Low bias, low variance

questions.explanation: Run-to-run spread is variance; good average performance indicates low bias.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the backpropagation algorithm?

options.A: It updates the weights directly
options.B: It computes the forward pass
options.C: It initializes the network
options.D: It computes all gradients required for optimizing the network

questions.explanation: Backprop applies the chain rule to obtain gradients; the optimizer then performs the update.

questions.question: A friend proposes the activation f(x)=0 for x<-0.5, x on [-0.5,0.5], 1 for x>0.5 and claims SGD trains well with it. What is the problem?

options.A: The gradient vanishes almost everywhere outside [-0.5, 0.5]
options.B: It is not continuous
options.C: It outputs values above 1
options.D: It cannot be evaluated on GPUs

questions.explanation: Outside the linear part the derivative is exactly zero, so saturated units get no learning signal.

questions.question: A network block computes ŷ = σ₂(x + w₂·σ₁(b₁ + w₁x)). Which architecture does this resemble?

options.A: VGG
options.B: A ResNet block
options.C: An LSTM cell
options.D: LeNet

questions.explanation: The '+ x' is a skip (identity) connection around the transformation — the ResNet idea.

questions.question: For a binary classifier whose single output should lie between 0 and 1, which final activation is appropriate?

options.A: Tanh
options.B: ReLU
options.C: Softmax
options.D: Sigmoid

questions.explanation: A scalar probability needs the sigmoid; softmax on one scalar always outputs 1.

questions.question: Which loss matches binary classification with a sigmoid output f(x) and label y ∈ {0,1}?

options.A: L = -y ln f(x) - (1-y) ln(1 - f(x))
options.B: L = max(0, f(x) - y)
options.D: L = (f(x) - y)⁴

questions.explanation: This is binary cross-entropy, the maximum-likelihood loss under a Bernoulli model.

questions.question: A confusion matrix shows 980 true positives, 18 false positives, 1 false negative, 1 true negative (1000 samples). The accuracy is:
questions.explanation: (980+1)/1000 = 98.1% — and it hides the near-total failure on the negative class.

questions.question: Bad design or fine? A binary classifier ends with ReLU, then a sigmoid, and everything with sigmoid ≥ 0.5 is 'positive'.

options.A: Bad — ReLU output is ≥ 0, so the sigmoid is always ≥ 0.5 and everything is classified positive
options.B: Bad — sigmoid cannot follow ReLU technically
options.C: Fine — the threshold adapts during training
options.D: Fine — probabilities are guaranteed

questions.explanation: sigmoid(z) ≥ 0.5 for all z ≥ 0; after ReLU there are no negative inputs left.

questions.question: Training loss curves for the same task: which noise ordering is expected?

options.A: Mini-batch is always noisiest
options.B: All three look identical
options.C: Full-batch noisiest, SGD smoothest
options.D: SGD noisiest, mini-batch in between, full-batch smoothest

questions.explanation: Smaller batches give noisier gradient estimates; the full batch gives the smoothest curve.

questions.question: You train with FULL-batch gradient descent and the data is not shuffled. Does shuffling help?

options.A: No — each update uses the whole training set, so the order within it is irrelevant
options.B: Yes, but only for images
options.C: Yes, it changes every update
options.D: No, shuffling is never useful in deep learning

questions.explanation: Order matters for stochastic/mini-batch updates, not when the full set is averaged per step.

questions.question: A conditional GAN is trained with mini-batches where ALL dog images come first, then all cat images. A colleague says you must shuffle. Right?

options.A: No, GANs are order-invariant
options.B: No, shuffling only matters for full-batch training
options.C: Yes, but only because of batch normalization
options.D: Yes — with sorted data the loss (and the generator's output) shifts drastically between class blocks, making optimization much harder

questions.explanation: Mini-batch updates follow the current batch distribution; class-sorted streams cause drift and instability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of momentum in optimizers?

options.A: It manipulates the learning rate to avoid exploding gradients
options.B: It averages samples inside a batch
options.C: It stabilizes training by keeping a moving average over previous gradients
options.D: It measures training stability

questions.explanation: Momentum accumulates past gradient directions, accelerating consistent directions and damping oscillation.

questions.question: What distinguishes Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) from classical momentum?

options.A: It uses no learning rate
options.B: It evaluates the gradient at a look-ahead position (after the momentum step) instead of the current weights
options.C: It removes the momentum term entirely
options.D: It only works with full-batch training

questions.explanation: NAG 'peeks ahead' along the velocity before computing the gradient, giving a correction effect.

questions.question: For an already-trained network f and an image x with label y=0, an adversarial example is created by optimizing:

options.A: The learning rate
options.B: The weights of f to minimize ||f(x)||²
options.C: The input x itself, e.g. minimizing L = ||f(x) - y||² = ||f(x)||² w.r.t. x
options.D: A new dataset

questions.explanation: Adversarial attacks hold the weights fixed and take gradient steps on the input.

questions.question: Minimizing binary cross-entropy corresponds to maximum-likelihood estimation under which distribution?

options.A: Uniform
options.B: Gaussian
options.C: Bernoulli
options.D: Poisson

questions.explanation: Two mutually exclusive outcomes with probability p and 1-p follow a Bernoulli distribution.

questions.question: Which final activation should be used for a multi-class problem with one-hot-encoded, mutually exclusive labels?

options.A: Sigmoid
options.B: Softmax
options.C: LeakyReLU
options.D: ReLU

questions.explanation: Softmax produces a probability distribution over the classes that sums to 1.

questions.question: The same model must predict 'contains horse' AND 'contains dog' (both can be true). Which output activation is suitable?

options.A: One sigmoid per output, since the labels are not mutually exclusive
options.B: ReLU
options.C: Argmax
options.D: Softmax over the two outputs

questions.explanation: Multi-label problems need independent per-class probabilities; softmax would force competition.

questions.question: Predictions with threshold 0.5 — image a: p(Horse)=0.8 (true: yes), b: p=0.4 (true: yes), c: p=0.7 (true: yes). The accuracy for class 'Horse' is:
questions.explanation: Predictions yes/no/yes vs truth yes/yes/yes → two of three correct.

questions.question: Which statement about the softmax as final layer is FALSE?

options.A: It produces one vector per sample for multi-class problems
options.B: The outputs for one sample depend on each other
options.C: It cannot be used during testing
options.D: It assigns a probability to each class

questions.explanation: Softmax is used at test time as well; only the loss computation is dropped.

questions.question: Which statement about implementing a convolutional layer is FALSE?

options.A: A stride larger than one reduces the spatial size
options.B: A 'valid' convolution outputs an image of the same size as its input
options.C: The number of kernel weights is independent of the image size
options.D: The number of kernels determines the number of output channels

questions.explanation: 'Valid' mode shrinks the output by kernel_size-1; 'same' padding keeps the size.

questions.question: Which statements about a convolutional layer are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: The total number of weights depends on the number of input channels
options.B: It can process inputs of arbitrary spatial size
options.C: The total number of weights depends on the stride
options.D: It computes rotation-invariant features

questions.explanation: Kernel weights scale with input channels; stride only affects the output size. Convolutions are translation-equivariant, not rotation-invariant.

questions.question: Which properties do convolutional networks exploit compared to fully connected ones? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Full connectivity between all pixels
options.B: The grid-like structure of images
options.C: Local connectivity
options.D: Shared weights

questions.explanation: Convolutions reuse a small local kernel across positions, matching image structure.

questions.question: For a 2-D convolution with odd kernel width k and stride 1, 'same' output size requires padding per side of:

options.B: 2k
options.D: k

questions.explanation: Half the kernel overhang on each side: n_p = (k-1)/2 for odd k.

questions.question: A stride-s convolution is implemented as a stride-1 convolution followed by subsampling. What must the backward pass do?

options.A: Transpose the kernels
options.B: Pad the incoming error tensor with zeros at the rows/columns that were discarded in the forward pass
options.C: Average the error tensor over the stride window
options.D: Nothing special

questions.explanation: Discarded positions received no output, so their gradient contribution is zero — insert zeros there.

questions.question: In max pooling, how is the gradient routed in the backward pass?

options.A: Evenly to all positions of the window
options.B: To the smallest element
options.C: It is discarded
options.D: Entirely to the position that held the maximum in the forward pass

questions.explanation: Only the argmax influenced the output, so it receives the full gradient; the rest get zero.

questions.question: A (10,10,3) image: option 1 flattens it and uses a fully connected layer with 5 outputs; option 2 uses a conv layer with five 10×10 kernels in 'valid' mode. Which is true?

options.A: Option 1 has more parameters
options.B: Both are mathematically equivalent
options.C: Option 2 has more parameters
options.D: Option 2 cannot produce 5 outputs

questions.explanation: A full-size valid kernel per output neuron is exactly a fully connected layer in disguise.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 1×1 convolution ('bottleneck layer')?

options.A: It computes inner products per position and can reduce the channel dimension, saving parameters and computation
options.B: It replaces the activation function
options.C: It performs pooling
options.D: It increases the spatial resolution

questions.explanation: 1×1 convolutions mix channels pointwise — a cheap, learnable dimensionality reduction.

questions.question: A CONV-3-32 layer ('same' padding, stride 1) processes a 32×32×3 input. How many weights does it have (ignoring biases)?
questions.explanation: Each of the 32 kernels spans 3 input channels with a 3×3 window: 32·3·9.

questions.question: What are feature maps in a CNN?

options.A: The activations generated by applying the kernels
options.B: The loss values per pixel
options.C: The kernels themselves
options.D: The pooling indices

questions.explanation: Each kernel produces one activation map showing where its pattern responds.

questions.question: What is the difference between convolution and cross-correlation?

options.A: Cross-correlation only works in 1-D
options.B: There is none
options.C: Convolution requires square kernels
options.D: Convolution flips the kernel; cross-correlation does not

questions.explanation: A convolution equals a cross-correlation with a flipped kernel and vice versa — deep learning frameworks typically implement cross-correlation.

questions.question: Which statements about batch normalization are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It normalizes the input distribution of the following layer
options.B: It has trainable parameters
options.C: It can be skipped during inference
options.D: It normalizes the weights of each layer

questions.explanation: BN standardizes activations and rescales them with learnable γ and β; at test time it runs with stored statistics.

questions.question: Which effect is credited to batch normalization during training?

options.A: It removes all overfitting
options.B: It eliminates the need for a learning rate
options.C: It smooths the loss landscape
options.D: It makes the model convex

questions.explanation: A smoother optimization landscape allows higher learning rates and faster convergence.

questions.question: How does batch normalization behave differently between training and testing?

options.A: Testing recomputes statistics on the test set
options.B: It is disabled during testing
options.C: Training uses batch statistics and updates a moving average; testing uses the stored (training) statistics
options.D: There is no difference

questions.explanation: Per-batch statistics would make test predictions depend on the batch — hence frozen moving averages.

questions.question: Comparing weight histograms after training: run 1 shows a sharp peak at exactly 0, run 2 shows a narrower Gaussian than the unregularized run. Which norms were used?

options.A: Run 1: L2, run 2: L1
options.B: Run 1: L1, run 2: L2
options.C: Both L2
options.D: Both L1

questions.explanation: L1 drives weights exactly to zero (sparsity); L2 shrinks all weights, reducing the variance.

questions.question: How does the keep probability in dropout relate to the strength of regularization?

options.A: The keep probability has no influence
options.B: Dropout does not regularize
options.C: Higher keep probability means stronger regularization
options.D: Lower keep probability (dropping more units) means stronger regularization

questions.explanation: Dropping more units forces more redundancy — keep probability 1.0 disables dropout entirely.

questions.question: Histograms of activations drift away from mean 0 / variance 1 layer by layer although inputs were normalized. Name the problem and a counter-measure.

options.A: Mode collapse — use minibatch discrimination
options.B: Overfitting — collect more data
options.C: Internal covariate shift — add batch normalization layers
options.D: Vanishing gradients — remove all activations

questions.explanation: The shifting layer-input distribution is countered by normalizing activations inside the network.

questions.question: What is the general purpose of regularization?

options.A: To increase model capacity
options.B: To speed up inference
options.C: To trade a bit of training fit for better generalization on unseen data
options.D: To reach zero training loss faster

questions.explanation: Regularization counters overfitting — the bias/variance trade-off in action.

questions.question: Which statements about adversarial examples are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: They also exist for other ML methods such as SVMs
options.B: They are generated by GANs
options.C: They cannot occur in real photographs
options.D: A slightly perturbed input can yield a completely different prediction

questions.explanation: Adversarial fragility is a general phenomenon of learned decision boundaries, not a GAN artifact.

questions.question: Which sampling strategy for generating sequences from a trained RNN does NOT exist?

options.A: Beam search
options.B: Recognition sampling
options.C: Greedy sampling
options.D: Random sampling

questions.explanation: Greedy, beam and random sampling are standard; 'recognition sampling' is made up.

questions.question: What role does the hidden state play in an Elman RNN?

options.A: It is only used during testing
options.B: It carries time-dependent context — a short-term memory connecting time steps
options.C: It stores the weights
options.D: It holds the loss value

questions.explanation: The hidden state is what makes the network recurrent: information flows from step to step.

questions.question: What advantage does an LSTM have over a simple RNN?

options.A: Fewer parameters
options.B: It processes images faster
options.C: It needs no training
options.D: A cell state that serves as long-term memory, making long dependencies easier to learn

questions.explanation: The gated cell state provides an almost unimpeded gradient path across many time steps.

questions.question: During TBPTT, why is it incorrect to update the hidden-gate weights WHILE iterating backwards through the time series?

options.A: The optimizer would diverge
options.B: Weights may only be updated once per epoch
options.C: The cell shares its weights across all time steps — changing them mid-backward changes the function being differentiated, so the gradients no longer match
options.D: The learning rate would need rescaling

questions.explanation: The remedy is to accumulate the gradients over time and apply one update after the backward pass.

questions.question: Truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) addresses which problem, and how?

options.A: The high cost of a full-sequence update — by splitting long sequences into smaller, overlapping chunks
options.B: Overfitting — by dropping time steps randomly
options.C: Exploding gradients — by clipping
options.D: Class imbalance — by resampling

questions.explanation: Updating only after a full long sequence is expensive; TBPTT updates on overlapping sub-sequences.

questions.question: Which problems are associated with the simple Elman cell? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Small weights multiplied over many steps can cause vanishing gradients
options.B: Long-term memory loss
options.C: Skip connections cause memory loss
options.D: Many-to-many mappings are impossible

questions.explanation: Repeated multiplication with the same weights shrinks (or explodes) gradients, and old context fades.

questions.question: In a recurrent cell without a bounded non-linearity, all inputs xₜ > 0 and weights w_hh, w_xh > 0. For long sequences one risks:

options.A: Underflow of the loss
options.B: Nothing, the setup is safe
options.C: Vanishing outputs
options.D: Exploding gradients — a tanh in a standard Elman cell would bound the state to (-1,1)

questions.explanation: Positive weights repeatedly multiplied grow without bound; the tanh in standard cells limits the state.

questions.question: A binary-cross-entropy loss is attached directly to a recurrent cell whose output is not bounded. What problem occurs?

options.A: Nothing
options.B: The cell forgets its state
options.C: BCE only works for images
options.D: The logarithm cannot handle values outside [0,1] — a sigmoid must squash the outputs first

questions.explanation: ln(ŷ) requires ŷ in (0,1); without a sigmoid the loss is undefined for out-of-range outputs.

questions.question: Which application is a natural fit for a recurrent neural network?

options.A: Sorting a list of numbers
options.B: Analyzing ECG signals, where neighboring samples are correlated in time
options.C: Computing image histograms
options.D: Classifying single, independent images

questions.explanation: RNNs shine when successive samples carry temporal dependencies (ECG, weather, language).

questions.question: In an encoder-decoder network, which part can be used on its own to create synthetic samples?

options.A: The decoder of a VAE
options.B: The encoder of a VAE
options.C: The discriminator of a GAN
options.D: The pooling layers

questions.explanation: Sampling the latent distribution and decoding generates new data; the VAE decoder is the generator.

questions.question: In an autoencoder figure, the part that maps the input to the bottleneck and the part that reconstructs from it are called:

options.A: Encoder and decoder
options.B: Generator and discriminator
options.C: Teacher and student
options.D: Actor and critic

questions.explanation: Encoder compresses, decoder reconstructs — the bottleneck in between is the code.

questions.question: What is the main difference between U-Nets and plain autoencoders?

options.A: U-Nets add skip connections from encoder to decoder levels
options.B: U-Nets have no decoder
options.C: U-Nets require labels for reconstruction
options.D: Autoencoders cannot use convolutions

questions.explanation: The skips carry high-resolution detail past the bottleneck — crucial for segmentation.

questions.question: How is a DENOISING autoencoder trained?

options.A: On labels from a human annotator
options.B: The input is corrupted with a noise model (e.g. Gaussian) and the network learns to reconstruct the original
options.C: With a discriminator
options.D: By maximizing the noise

questions.explanation: Reconstructing clean data from corrupted input forces robust representations — and enables in-painting.

questions.question: What distinguishes a VARIATIONAL autoencoder from a standard one?

options.A: It requires labeled data
options.B: It uses no decoder
options.C: It compresses inputs into a constrained latent DISTRIBUTION (e.g. Gaussian) instead of a free latent vector
options.D: It has no bottleneck

questions.explanation: The latent space is regularized toward a known distribution, so sampling from it yields valid new data.

questions.question: A linear autoencoder (no non-linearities) trained with L2 loss learns a generalization of:

options.A: K-means
options.B: Softmax regression
options.C: A decision tree
options.D: PCA

questions.explanation: Without non-linearities the optimal bottleneck spans the principal subspace of the data.

questions.question: How is a GAN trained?

options.A: With cross-validation
options.B: Only the generator is trained
options.C: A min-max game: the discriminator maximizes classification of real vs. fake, the generator minimizes the discriminator's success
options.D: Generator and discriminator minimize the same loss together

questions.explanation: Generator maps noise to samples; discriminator judges real vs. generated — adversarial objectives.

questions.question: You want users to pick 'forest', 'lake', 'mountains' etc. and get matching generated images. Which GAN type and training data do you need?

options.A: A conditional GAN — both networks receive a class encoding, and the training images need environment labels
options.B: A GAN trained on noise alone
options.C: A discriminator only
options.D: A vanilla GAN with unlabeled images

questions.explanation: Conditioning inputs steer generation; training therefore needs (image, label) pairs.

questions.question: After 100 GAN epochs the loss values look like at the start. Must you assume nothing was learned?

options.A: No, losses are meaningless in general
options.B: No — generator and discriminator may both have improved equally well, keeping the losses balanced
options.C: Yes, GAN losses must go to zero
options.D: Yes, constant loss always means no progress

questions.explanation: In the adversarial game, mutual improvement can leave the equilibrium losses unchanged.

questions.question: Test users complain that all images from your GAN look nearly identical. What happened, and what is a counter-strategy?

options.A: Overfitting — early stopping
options.B: Mode collapse — mini-batch discrimination or unrolled GANs
options.C: Vanishing gradients — ReLU
options.D: Data leakage — new test split

questions.explanation: The generator found one output family that fools the discriminator; diversity penalties counteract it.

questions.question: Which architectures contain BOTH convolutional layers and skip connections? (Select all that apply)

options.A: ResNet
options.B: VGG16
options.C: LeNet
options.D: U-Net

questions.explanation: U-Net skips encoder features to the decoder; ResNet adds identity shortcuts. LeNet/VGG have neither.

questions.question: Which features are NOT used in LeNet? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Skip connections
options.B: Convolutional layers
options.C: Pooling layers
options.D: ReLU non-linearities

questions.explanation: LeNet used convolutions, subsampling and tanh/sigmoid — ReLU and skips came later.

questions.question: What are advantages of making a network deeper? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Increasingly abstract features
options.B: Less memory consumption
options.C: Ability to approximate increasingly complex functions
options.D: Exponential feature reuse

questions.explanation: Depth builds feature hierarchies and expressiveness — at the cost of memory, not in its favor.

questions.question: Roughly how does model capacity relate to a network's design?

options.A: It is fixed by the number of training samples
options.B: It is linked to the variety of functions the network can approximate and grows with depth/parameters
options.C: It is unrelated to architecture
options.D: It only depends on the optimizer

questions.explanation: Capacity describes the richness of representable functions — closely tied to the bias/variance trade-off.

questions.question: A pretrained CNN ends with flatten + fully connected layers and was trained on 32×32 images. To process arbitrarily-sized images while keeping a length-10 output, you should:

options.A: Add more FC layers
options.B: Replace flatten + FC with 1×1 (or n×n) convolutions and global average pooling
options.C: Resize all images to 32×32 forever
options.D: Remove all convolutions

questions.explanation: Convolutional heads are size-agnostic; global pooling collapses any spatial extent to a fixed vector.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a technique to visualize important image areas of a classifier?

options.A: Inception
options.B: Occlusion
options.C: Saliency maps
options.D: Guided backpropagation

questions.explanation: Inception is an architecture module, not a visualization method.

questions.question: How are saliency maps generated, and what serves as the pseudo loss?

options.A: The classification score of the target class is used as pseudo loss and its gradient w.r.t. the input is visualized
options.B: The input is blurred repeatedly
options.C: The weights are plotted directly
options.D: The accuracy is backpropagated

questions.explanation: Backpropagating the class score to the input shows which pixels influence the decision most.

questions.question: Your saliency maps show the network ignores the face for smile detection. Training it additionally on related tasks (glasses detection, pose) is called:

options.A: Bagging
options.B: Curriculum learning
options.C: Multi-task learning with auxiliary tasks
options.D: Distillation

questions.explanation: Auxiliary tasks share features and can steer attention toward the relevant regions.

questions.question: In reinforcement learning, what is the difference between exploitation and exploration?

options.A: Exploitation samples random actions
options.B: There is none
options.C: Exploitation uses the currently known best action; exploration tries potentially suboptimal new actions to find better returns
options.D: Exploration is only used during testing

questions.explanation: Balancing both is essential — pure exploitation gets stuck in locally optimal behavior.

questions.question: Which statement about reinforcement learning is CORRECT?

options.A: Exploiting one known good action suffices during training
options.B: Exploration should be used during training to try out new action sequences
options.C: Exploitation enforces new action sequences
options.D: Exploration should be used during testing

questions.explanation: Training needs exploration (e.g. epsilon-greedy); at test time the learned policy is exploited.

questions.question: Which statement about RL methods is WRONG?

options.A: A good policy aims to maximize the future return
options.B: Temporal-difference learning is an on-policy method that needs information about the environment dynamics
options.C: The Bellman equations form a linear system solvable for small problems
options.D: Q-learning is an off-policy method that needs no model of the environment dynamics

questions.explanation: TD learning is model-free — it learns from sampled transitions without knowing the dynamics.

questions.question: In epsilon-greedy action selection, the agent:

options.A: Always takes the greedy action
options.B: Takes a random action with probability ε and the currently best-valued action otherwise
options.C: Follows the reward gradient
options.D: Always acts randomly

questions.explanation: ε controls the exploration rate; the rest of the time the highest Q-value action is exploited.

questions.question: Why does a purely greedy policy on immediate rewards fail in grid-world style tasks?

options.A: Greedy policies are not computable
options.B: Because rewards are always negative
options.C: It only exploits the next action's reward and cannot look ahead over multiple steps
options.D: It explores too much

questions.explanation: Maximizing one-step reward ignores state transitions and long-term return.

questions.question: In the RL interaction loop, the agent sends ___ to the environment and receives ___ back.

options.A: actions; the next state and a reward
options.B: states; actions
options.C: gradients; weights
options.D: rewards; losses

questions.explanation: Agent acts (aₜ); environment answers with state sₜ₊₁ and reward rₜ₊₁.

questions.question: What are the two main tasks of object detection?

options.A: Super-resolution of regions of interest
options.B: Boundary search between objects
options.C: In-painting missing image parts
options.D: Finding bounding boxes and classifying them

questions.explanation: Detection = where (localization via boxes) + what (class per box).

questions.question: Which statement about YOLO vs. (Fast) R-CNN is TRUE?

options.A: Fast R-CNN does real-time detection while YOLO cannot
options.B: YOLO combines bounding-box prediction and classification in ONE network
options.C: YOLO produces candidate regions that are passed to a CNN
options.D: Fast R-CNN is generally faster

questions.explanation: That single forward pass is why YOLO is called a single-shot detector and runs in real time.

questions.question: Why is YOLO called a 'Single-Shot Detector' (SSD)?

options.A: It combines box prediction and classification in one model and one forward pass
options.B: It needs a single training image
options.C: It only detects one object
options.D: It uses a single pixel

questions.explanation: No separate proposal stage — everything happens in one pass.

questions.question: Which statements are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Semantic segmentation can be considered pixel-wise classification
options.B: The goal of segmentation is drawing bounding boxes
options.C: Instance segmentation can only find one instance per class
options.D: A pixel-wise segmentation of an object can be converted into a bounding box

questions.explanation: Segmentation labels pixels; boxes follow from the mask extent. Instance segmentation separates many instances.

questions.question: You must check whether the number of people in a room is below capacity. Semantic segmentation or object detection?

options.A: Semantic segmentation, because it labels every pixel
options.B: Only classification
options.C: Object detection, because detecting person instances allows counting them
options.D: Neither works

questions.explanation: Counting needs instances; semantic masks of 'person' pixels merge people together.

questions.question: In segmentation evaluation with pᵢⱼ = pixels of true class i predicted as class j, the metric Σᵢ pᵢᵢ / Σᵢ Σⱼ pᵢⱼ is:

options.A: Mean class-wise pixel accuracy
options.B: Pixel Accuracy
options.C: Mean Intersection over Union
options.D: Frequency Weighted IoU

questions.explanation: Correct pixels over all pixels — the plain pixel accuracy.

questions.question: With the same notation, the per-class Intersection over Union of class i is:

options.A: pᵢᵢ / Σⱼ pᵢⱼ
options.B: Σⱼ pᵢⱼ / pᵢᵢ
options.C: pᵢᵢ / (Σⱼ pᵢⱼ + Σⱼ pⱼᵢ − pᵢᵢ)
options.D: pᵢᵢ · Σⱼ pⱼᵢ

questions.explanation: Intersection pᵢᵢ over union = predicted ∪ ground-truth pixels of the class (counting the overlap once).

questions.question: Which metrics are commonly used to evaluate semantic segmentation? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Perplexity
options.B: Mean Intersection over Union
options.C: Frequency Weighted IoU
options.D: Pixel Accuracy

questions.explanation: Perplexity is a language-model metric; the others are standard for segmentation.

questions.question: Why are fully connected layers on flattened pixels ill-suited for segmenting street scenes?

options.A: They are not translation invariant and need vastly more parameters than convolutions
options.B: They cannot output more than one value
options.C: They cannot be trained with SGD
options.D: They require grayscale input

questions.explanation: Reoccurring objects at different positions need shared, local filters — exactly what convolutions provide.

questions.question: Why is it useful to learn a bias term in addition to the weights?

options.A: It normalises the input distribution
options.B: It prevents the weights from growing too large
options.C: It guarantees the activations stay positive
options.D: It shifts the activation function, so the decision boundary need not pass through the origin

questions.explanation: Without a bias, every layer computes a purely linear map through the origin, which severely restricts the functions the network can represent.

questions.question: What does the model capacity of a network describe?

options.A: The variety of functions it can approximate
options.B: The maximum batch size it can process
options.C: The number of training samples it was optimised with
options.D: The amount of GPU memory it occupies

questions.explanation: Capacity is a property of the model class, not of the dataset; it grows with depth and width and sits at the centre of the bias-variance trade-off.

questions.question: Which statements about model capacity are TRUE? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Increasing capacity necessarily increases the bias of the prediction
options.B: It is closely related to the bias-variance trade-off
options.C: It is linked to the variety of functions that can be approximated
options.D: It is influenced by the depth of the network

questions.explanation: Higher capacity typically lowers bias and raises variance, so the last statement has the relationship backwards.

questions.question: You feed a network trained on ImageNet a blank image where every pixel has the same white value. Will it output the same score for every class?

options.A: No, because the weights and biases differ per class, so a constant input still produces different scores
options.B: Yes, because a constant input carries no class information
options.C: Yes, but only if the last layer is a SoftMax
options.D: It cannot be determined without knowing the architecture

questions.explanation: A constant input is still multiplied by different weight vectors, so the logits differ; uniform output would require identical weights per class.

questions.question: In a network with two fully connected layers and no non-linearity between them, why is the model no more expressive than a single layer?

options.A: Because the composition of two linear maps is again a linear map, W = W2 W1 with bias W2 b1 + b2
options.B: Because the second layer's weights are always zero
options.C: Because the batch dimension collapses
options.D: Because the gradients cancel out

questions.explanation: Only a non-linearity between the layers lets the network represent functions a single affine map cannot.

questions.question: Data normalisation example: a feature has mean 82 and standard deviation 40, and the observed value is 22. What is the normalised value?
questions.explanation: Standardisation subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation: (22 - 82) / 40 = -1.5.

questions.question: A two-feature dataset is normalised to (-1, -1), (-1, 1), (1, -1), (1, 1) with alternating labels along the diagonals. Was a single-layer perceptron a good choice?

options.A: No, the distribution is an XOR problem, which a single-layer perceptron cannot solve
options.B: No, because the data must first be augmented
options.C: Yes, the data is linearly separable after normalisation
options.D: Yes, because normalisation always makes data separable

questions.explanation: XOR requires at least one hidden layer; no single hyperplane separates the two diagonal pairs.

questions.question: How do you connect the output of a convolutional layer to the input of a fully connected layer?

options.A: By adding a second convolutional layer with kernel size equal to the image size
options.B: By transposing the feature maps
options.C: By averaging all channels into a single scalar
options.D: By inserting a flatten layer that reshapes the feature maps into a vector

questions.explanation: Flattening (or global pooling) turns the multi-dimensional feature maps into the one-dimensional vector the fully connected layer expects.

questions.question: A network flattens a feature map and applies a fully connected layer. A colleague replaces this with a convolution whose kernel covers the whole feature map. How do the two compare?

options.A: The fully connected version is translation invariant, the convolutional one is not
options.B: The convolution has far fewer parameters
options.C: The convolution cannot produce the same output
options.D: They are equivalent; the convolutional version avoids the explicit flatten and may be implemented more efficiently

questions.explanation: A convolution whose kernel spans the entire input is exactly a fully connected layer written in convolutional form.

questions.question: An image is (X, X) and a fully connected layer produces Z outputs. Ignoring the bias, how many weights does it need?

options.A: X^2 + Z
options.B: X * Z
options.C: X^2 * Z
options.D: X * Z^2

questions.explanation: Every one of the X^2 input pixels connects to every one of the Z output neurons.

questions.question: The same image is processed by a convolutional layer with N kernels of size (K, K) on a single input channel. Ignoring bias, how many weights does it need?

options.A: N * K^2
options.B: X^2 * N
options.D: N * K

questions.explanation: Weight sharing makes the parameter count independent of the image size, which is the key advantage over the fully connected layer.

questions.question: For a 2-D convolution with an EVEN filter width k and stride 1, what padding gives an output of the same size as the input?

options.A: Same size output is impossible for even k
options.B: (k-1)/2 on both sides
options.C: k/2 on both sides
options.D: Asymmetric padding: k/2 - 1 on one side and k/2 on the other

questions.explanation: Odd kernels allow the symmetric (k-1)/2; an even kernel has no centre pixel, so the two sides must differ by one.

questions.question: Which statement about a 'valid' convolution is FALSE?

options.A: It produces a smaller output than a 'same' convolution with the same kernel
options.B: It discards border positions where the kernel does not fully overlap the input
options.C: It applies no padding
options.D: It outputs an image of the same size as its input

questions.explanation: That describes 'same' convolution. A valid convolution shrinks the output by k-1 in each spatial dimension.

questions.question: A CONV layer has 16 kernels applied to an input with 3 channels. How many channels does the output have?
questions.explanation: Each kernel spans all input channels and produces exactly one output feature map, so the number of kernels sets the output depth.

questions.question: A pytorch model applies nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3, padding=1) to an input of shape 256 x 256 x 3, and later maps back to 3 channels with the same padding. What is the output shape?
questions.explanation: A 3x3 kernel with padding 1 and stride 1 preserves the spatial size, so only the channel count changes through the network.

questions.question: Why is a stride greater than 1 sometimes described as subsampling?

options.A: Because it reduces the number of channels
options.B: Because it applies the kernel to a random subset of positions
options.C: Because it averages neighbouring pixels
options.D: Because it evaluates the convolution only at every s-th position, discarding the intermediate outputs

questions.explanation: That equivalence is exactly why a strided convolution can be implemented as a stride-1 convolution followed by dropping rows and columns.

questions.question: A strided convolution is implemented as stride-1 convolution plus subsampling. What must happen to the error tensor in the backward pass?

options.A: It must be averaged over the stride window
options.B: It must be scaled by the stride
options.C: It must be transposed
options.D: It must be padded with zeros at the rows and columns that were discarded in the forward pass

questions.explanation: Positions that produced no output receive no gradient, so inserting zeros restores the shape the stride-1 backward pass expects.

questions.question: Name a plausible cause of internal covariate shift.

options.A: The distribution of a layer's inputs changes as the parameters of the preceding layers are updated
options.B: The learning rate is too small
options.C: The loss function is non-convex
options.D: The test set is drawn from a different population than the training set

questions.explanation: Because every layer's input is the previous layer's output, updating early layers shifts the distribution that later layers have to cope with.

questions.question: Which situations contribute to internal covariate shift? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Weight updates in earlier layers changing the distribution seen by later layers
options.B: Large learning rates causing large parameter changes per step
options.C: Unnormalised input data with widely differing feature scales
options.D: Using a fixed random seed

questions.explanation: The seed only fixes the initialisation and shuffling order; it does not affect how distributions drift during training.

questions.question: You train one network with L2 regularization and one with L1. How do the weights differ from an unregularised run?

options.A: Both give identical weight distributions
options.B: L1 increases the weights, L2 decreases them
options.C: L2 gives sparser weights, L1 gives uniformly smaller weights
options.D: L2 gives smaller weights overall, L1 gives sparser weights with many close to zero

questions.explanation: The quadratic penalty shrinks large weights hardest, whereas the absolute-value penalty has constant gradient magnitude and drives small weights to exactly zero.

questions.question: Which observation about a trained weight histogram most strongly suggests L1 rather than L2 regularization?

options.A: A bimodal distribution with peaks at -1 and 1
options.B: A uniform distribution over [-1, 1]
options.C: A sharp spike exactly at zero with a spread of non-zero weights around it
options.D: A narrow Gaussian centred at zero

questions.explanation: L2 shrinks everything smoothly towards zero, so it yields a narrow bell shape rather than an atom of mass exactly at zero.

questions.question: Three loss-over-iteration curves are observed for the same task, with noise levels high, medium and almost none. Which ordering matches SGD, mini-batch GD and batch GD?

options.A: SGD is the noisiest, mini-batch is intermediate, batch GD is the smoothest
options.B: All three are equally noisy
options.C: Batch GD is the noisiest, SGD the smoothest
options.D: Mini-batch GD is the noisiest

questions.explanation: The gradient estimate averages over more samples as the batch grows, so its variance and therefore the curve noise shrink.

questions.question: A GAN is trained with mini-batches in which all dog images come first, then all cat images. Why is this a problem?

options.A: It is not a problem as long as every image is seen once per epoch
options.B: The batches are too large
options.C: The discriminator cannot process two classes
options.D: Each mini-batch gradient is biased towards one class, so the updates oscillate instead of approximating the full-data gradient

questions.explanation: Mini-batch gradient descent assumes each batch is a representative sample; sorted batches break that assumption badly.

questions.question: Why does a classifier that ends with ReLU followed by sigmoid, thresholded at 0.5, classify every sample as positive?

options.A: Because the sigmoid saturates
options.B: Because the ReLU output is always >= 0, so the sigmoid output is always >= 0.5
options.C: Because the loss function is wrong
options.D: Because the weights were initialised with zeros

questions.explanation: sigmoid(0) = 0.5 and the function is monotonic, so a non-negative pre-activation can never fall below the threshold.

questions.question: Would a step-like activation that is 0 for x < -0.5, x on [-0.5, 0.5] and 1 for x > 0.5 train well with SGD?

options.A: Yes, because it is bounded
options.B: No, because it is not continuous
options.C: Yes, it behaves like a bounded ReLU
options.D: No, because the gradient is zero almost everywhere outside the narrow linear region

questions.explanation: Backpropagation multiplies by the derivative, so units driven into the flat regions stop receiving any learning signal.

questions.question: A simplified Elman cell has h_t = ReLU(w_hh * h_{t-1} + w_xh * x_t) with w_hh = 1, w_xh = 2, h_0 = 0 and x_1 = 2. What is h_1?
questions.explanation: The recurrent contribution is zero at t = 1, so h_1 = ReLU(2 * 2) = 4.

questions.question: Continuing that cell with x_2 = -1, w_hh = 1, w_xh = 2 and h_1 = 4, what is h_2?
questions.explanation: h_2 = ReLU(1 * 4 + 2 * (-1)) = ReLU(2) = 2, so the ReLU does not clip in this step.

questions.question: With w_hy = 0.5 and an output y_t = ReLU(w_hy * h_t), what is y_1 when h_1 = 4?
In a recurrent cell with only ReLU non-linearities, all inputs positive and all weights positive, what goes wrong for long sequences, and how does a standard Elman cell differ?

options.A: Vanishing gradients; the standard cell is worse because tanh saturates
options.B: The hidden state becomes constant; the standard cell has the same issue
options.C: Nothing goes wrong; ReLU cells are preferred for long sequences
options.D: Exploding gradients; a standard Elman cell passes the state through tanh, which bounds it and instead risks vanishing gradients

questions.explanation: With no bounded non-linearity, repeated multiplication by weights greater than one makes the state and its gradient grow without limit.

questions.question: How would you fix a recurrent cell that suffers from exploding activations because all weights and inputs are positive?

options.A: Increase the learning rate
options.B: Use a bounded activation such as tanh or sigmoid, or initialise with a zero-centred distribution
options.C: Train for fewer iterations
options.D: Remove the recurrent connection

questions.explanation: Either bound the signal so it cannot grow, or make positive and negative contributions cancel on average.

questions.question: Why are recurrent neural networks suitable for time-series problems?

options.A: Because they are translation invariant
options.B: Because the hidden state carries information from earlier time steps, capturing temporal correlation
options.C: Because they need fewer parameters than fully connected networks
options.D: Because they process the whole sequence in parallel

questions.explanation: Neighbouring samples in a time series are correlated, and the recurrence is exactly the mechanism that lets earlier samples influence later predictions.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of an LSTM compared to an Elman cell?

options.A: Its gated cell state preserves gradients over long sequences, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem
options.B: It does not require backpropagation through time
options.C: It can process variable-length sequences, which the Elman cell cannot
options.D: It has fewer parameters

questions.explanation: The additive cell state gives gradients a route through time that is not repeatedly squashed by a saturating non-linearity.

questions.question: An architecture that maps an input to a compressed representation and reconstructs it is used for self-representation learning. What is it called?

options.A: A generative adversarial network
options.B: A residual network
options.C: An autoencoder
options.D: A U-Net

questions.explanation: Training targets the input itself, which makes it unsupervised, and the bottleneck forces a compact representation.

questions.question: How does a conditional GAN let a user request a specific kind of generated image?

options.A: By using a larger noise vector
options.B: By giving both generator and discriminator an additional input that encodes the target class
options.C: By retraining the generator for each request
options.D: By filtering the generated samples after training

questions.explanation: The training set must then contain labelled examples of each target category so the discriminator can learn the conditional distribution.

questions.question: Kernel weights are hard to interpret. What do you visualise instead to understand a convolutional layer?

options.A: The bias values
options.B: The gradients of the loss
options.C: The feature maps, i.e. the activations the layer produces for a given input
options.D: The learning rate schedule

questions.explanation: Activations show what the layer actually responds to for a concrete image, which is far more interpretable than the raw filter coefficients.

questions.question: Name a measure that can evaluate segmentation but not plain classification.

options.A: Accuracy
options.B: Intersection over Union
options.C: Precision
options.D: Cross-entropy loss

questions.explanation: IoU and the Dice coefficient compare predicted and true regions, which only makes sense when the prediction has spatial extent.

questions.question: Which statement about semantic segmentation is CORRECT?

options.A: It treats multiple objects of the same class as separate entities
options.B: It draws a bounding box around the object of interest
options.C: It can only find one object per image
options.D: It can differentiate between different classes in an image, assigning a class label per pixel

questions.explanation: Distinguishing separate objects of the same class is instance segmentation; bounding boxes are object detection.

questions.question: Can a pixel-wise segmentation be converted into a bounding box?

options.A: Only if the segmentation is binary
options.B: No, the two representations are incompatible
options.C: Yes, by taking the extent of the segmented region
options.D: Only for convex objects

questions.explanation: The conversion loses information and does not work in the other direction, which is why segmentation labels are the richer annotation.

questions.question: You must count the people in a room to check it is below capacity. Which task fits best?

options.A: Object detection, since it produces one instance per person and instances can be counted
options.B: Image classification, since the answer is a single number
options.C: Denoising, since the images are noisy
options.D: Semantic segmentation, since it labels every person pixel

questions.explanation: Semantic segmentation merges all people into one class region, so overlapping people cannot be told apart to be counted.

questions.question: A U-Net-like model is missing one key concept compared to the real U-Net. Which one?

options.A: Skip connections from the encoder to the decoder
options.B: Batch normalization
options.C: A softmax output
options.D: Dropout in the bottleneck

questions.explanation: Those connections carry high-resolution spatial detail that the downsampling path would otherwise discard.

questions.question: Why is classifying each pixel independently with fully connected layers a poor approach to segmentation? (Select all that apply)

options.A: The approach discards the spatial context around each pixel
options.B: The number of parameters is far larger than for a convolutional network
options.C: Fully connected layers cannot produce more than two output classes
options.D: Fully connected layers are not translation invariant, so an object at a new location is harder to segment

questions.explanation: The class count is not the issue; the problems are the loss of weight sharing, the parameter explosion and the missing spatial context.

questions.question: In which scenario would you use under- or oversampling?

options.A: To create artificial data with variational autoencoders
options.B: To evaluate a segmentation task
options.C: To counter class imbalance during training
options.D: Inside the dropout layer to boost the influence of certain weights

questions.explanation: Undersampling removes majority samples and oversampling repeats minority samples so the classes contribute more equally to the loss.

questions.question: How should the FINAL performance of a fine-tuned network be evaluated?

options.A: Mean precision over training, validation and test data combined
options.B: Accuracy on the validation set
options.C: A metric such as an ROC curve computed on the held-out test set
options.D: Recall on the training set

questions.explanation: The validation set guided model selection, so it is no longer unbiased; only untouched test data gives an honest estimate.

questions.question: A network trained on 32x32 images ends with flatten and fully connected layers. How do you adapt it to accept arbitrarily sized images and still output a vector of length 10?

options.A: Replace the flatten and fully connected layers with convolutional layers and average pooling
options.B: Resize every input to 32x32
options.C: Retrain the whole network from scratch
options.D: Add a recurrent layer at the end

questions.explanation: Convolutions have no fixed input size, and global average pooling collapses whatever spatial extent remains into a fixed-length vector.

questions.question: You write a data generator that serves mini-batches. The last batch of an epoch would contain only 3 of the requested 32 samples. What is the standard way to handle this?

options.A: Skip the batch and begin the next epoch immediately
options.B: Serve the short batch as it is, since the loss is averaged anyway
options.C: Complete the batch by wrapping around to samples from the start of the dataset
options.D: Pad the batch with zero images and zero labels

questions.explanation: Constant batch size keeps the variance of the gradient estimate stable across iterations. Padding with zeros would inject samples that are not real data.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about shuffling the training data is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: The data should be reshuffled at the start of every epoch, not shuffled once
options.B: Shuffling prevents the same sample from appearing twice within one epoch
options.C: Without shuffling, mini-batches drawn from sorted data give biased gradient estimates
options.D: Shuffling changes the value of the loss function being minimised

questions.explanation: Shuffling reorders samples, it does not deduplicate them - an epoch visits every sample exactly once either way. The loss is a sum over the whole training set and does not depend on visit order.

questions.question: You apply random rotation and mirroring to your training images. Which statement describes the correct way to apply them?

options.A: One transform is drawn per image, so a single batch may mix transformed and untransformed images
options.B: Transforms are applied to the validation set as well, to keep the distributions matched
options.C: One transform is drawn per batch and applied to every image in that batch
options.D: The transform is drawn once and reused for the whole epoch

questions.explanation: Per-image sampling is what makes augmentation increase the effective diversity of the data. Augmenting the validation set would make the reported score depend on random draws rather than on model quality.

questions.question: Your image augmentation restricts rotations to 90, 180 and 270 degrees rather than any angle in [0, 360). What is the main practical reason?

options.A: Those angles are the only ones that preserve the label
options.B: Convolutional layers are only invariant to multiples of 90 degrees
options.C: They map the pixel grid onto itself, so no interpolation and no empty corners are introduced
options.D: Arbitrary angles would change the number of channels

questions.explanation: An arbitrary rotation resamples the grid, blurring the image and leaving undefined corner regions that must be filled. Multiples of 90 degrees are exact permutations of the pixels.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about normalising input data is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Features on very different scales produce an elongated loss surface, so gradient descent zig-zags
options.B: A common choice is to transform the data to zero mean and unit variance
options.C: The test set must be normalised with the statistics computed on the training set
options.D: Each sample should be normalised independently so its own features are comparably scaled

questions.explanation: Normalising per sample would destroy genuine differences in magnitude between samples and would apply a different transform to training and test data. The statistics belong to the dataset, not to the sample.

questions.question: Why are array operations in a data pipeline written as vectorised expressions rather than as element-wise Python loops?

options.A: The interpreter overhead per element makes loops far slower than one vectorised call
options.B: Loops cannot index multi-dimensional arrays
options.C: Loops give numerically different results
options.D: Vectorised code is required for gradients to be computed

questions.explanation: A vectorised call dispatches once into compiled code that processes the whole buffer, while a Python loop pays interpreter overhead on every single element.

questions.question: What is the difference between resizing an image and reshaping it?

options.A: Resizing interpolates to a new pixel count, reshaping only reinterprets the existing values under a new shape
options.B: Reshaping interpolates, resizing only reorders
options.C: They are two names for the same operation
options.D: Resizing works on colour images, reshaping only on grayscale

questions.explanation: Reshaping requires the total number of elements to be unchanged; resizing changes it and must invent or discard information by interpolation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the forward and backward passes of a layer is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: The forward pass computes the layer's output from its input
options.B: The backward pass of a layer with parameters also computes the gradient with respect to those parameters
options.C: The backward pass returns the gradient with respect to the layer's input
options.D: The backward pass requires no information from the forward pass

questions.explanation: Most layers must cache something from the forward pass - a fully connected layer needs its input to form the weight gradient, and ReLU needs to know which entries were positive.

questions.question: A layer is called trainable if

options.A: it has parameters that an optimizer updates during training
options.B: it implements a backward pass
options.C: its output changes between training and testing
options.D: it appears before the loss in the network

questions.explanation: ReLU, pooling and flatten all implement a backward pass but hold no parameters. Behaving differently at test time is a separate property, held by dropout and batch normalization.

questions.question: A fully connected layer receives a batch of shape (batch_size, input_size). Which quantity must its forward pass store so the backward pass can compute the weight gradient?

options.A: The output of the layer
options.B: Nothing; the incoming error tensor is sufficient
options.C: The current learning rate
options.D: The input tensor

questions.explanation: The weight gradient is formed from the product of the stored input and the incoming error tensor, so the input must survive until the backward pass.

questions.question: A fully connected layer folds its bias into the weight matrix by appending a constant column of ones to the input. What is the benefit?

options.A: The affine transform becomes a single matrix multiplication instead of a multiplication plus an addition
options.B: The gradient with respect to the bias becomes zero
options.C: The bias is no longer learned, which reduces overfitting
options.D: The layer no longer needs an activation function

questions.explanation: The bias is still learned - it just lives in an extra row of the weight matrix, so one matrix product implements the whole affine map.

questions.question: You build a network as FullyConnected, FullyConnected, SoftMax, with no activation between the two fully connected layers. What is wrong with this design?

options.A: The composition of two affine maps is again affine, so the pair is no more expressive than a single fully connected layer
options.B: The gradients will vanish immediately
options.C: SoftMax cannot follow a fully connected layer
options.D: Nothing; two layers always express more than one

questions.explanation: Depth only buys expressive power when a non-linearity separates the layers. Without one, W2(W1x + b1) + b2 collapses into a single affine transform.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the ReLU activation is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It has no trainable parameters
options.B: Units whose input is always negative receive no gradient and stop learning
options.C: It squashes its output into the range (0, 1)
options.D: Its derivative is 1 for positive inputs, which limits the vanishing gradient problem

questions.explanation: The bounded (0,1) range describes the sigmoid. ReLU is unbounded above, which is exactly why it does not saturate for positive inputs.

questions.question: A SoftMax layer is placed at the end of a classification network. What does it produce?

options.A: The index of the predicted class
options.B: The loss value for the batch
options.C: One probability distribution over the classes per sample in the batch
options.D: A binary vector marking the correct class

questions.explanation: Each row is exponentiated and normalised so the entries are positive and sum to one, which is what lets them be read as class probabilities.

questions.question: During backpropagation, why does the cross-entropy loss receive the label tensor rather than an incoming error tensor?

options.A: Because the labels change on every iteration
options.B: Because the labels are needed to normalise the gradient
options.C: Because the loss has trainable parameters
options.D: Because the loss is where backpropagation starts, so no error has been propagated into it yet

questions.explanation: Every other layer is handed the gradient of the loss with respect to its own output. The loss sits at the top of the chain and forms that first gradient itself, from the predictions and the labels.

questions.question: What does a single call to an optimizer's update step do?

options.A: It computes the gradient of the loss with respect to the weights
options.B: It returns updated weights, given the current weights and their gradient
options.C: It runs a forward and a backward pass
options.D: It resets the accumulated gradients to zero

questions.explanation: Gradient computation belongs to the layers. The optimizer only decides how to turn a gradient into a parameter change, which is what lets SGD, momentum and Adam be swapped freely.

questions.question: You give one shared optimizer object to every layer of a network and train with a momentum method. Why is this a problem?

options.A: The learning rate would be applied twice per iteration
options.B: It is not a problem; momentum is stateless
options.C: Momentum keeps a per-parameter velocity, so one shared object mixes the states of unrelated layers
options.D: Momentum cannot be used with more than one layer

questions.explanation: Plain SGD is stateless and would survive this, but any method with internal state - momentum, Adam - needs its own copy per parameter set.

questions.question: You initialise every weight in a hidden layer to the same non-zero constant. What happens during training?

options.A: The bias terms compensate and break the symmetry
options.B: All units in the layer receive identical gradients and stay identical, so the layer behaves like a single unit
options.C: The gradients explode immediately
options.D: The layer trains normally but converges more slowly

questions.explanation: Breaking symmetry is the whole reason initialisation is random. Identical weights make units indistinguishable, and identical gradients keep them that way.

questions.question: Xavier (Glorot) initialisation sets the weight variance from both the number of inputs and the number of outputs of a layer. What is it trying to achieve?

options.A: That the variance of the signal is preserved in both the forward and the backward direction
options.B: That every unit receives the same gradient
options.C: That the weights are sparse
options.D: That the weights stay small enough for L2 regularization to be unnecessary

questions.explanation: If the variance shrinks layer after layer the signal dies out; if it grows the activations explode. Involving both fan-in and fan-out balances the two directions.

questions.question: He initialisation scales the weight variance by 2/fan_in rather than balancing fan_in and fan_out as Xavier does. Which design decision motivates the factor of 2?

options.A: The use of ReLU activations, which zero out roughly half the signal
options.B: The use of mini-batches rather than full-batch gradient descent
options.C: The use of the Adam optimizer
options.D: The presence of a bias term in each layer

questions.explanation: ReLU discards the negative half of its input, halving the variance of what it passes on, so the initial variance is doubled to compensate.

questions.question: For a convolutional layer, what does fan_in count?

options.A: The number of weights feeding one output unit: input channels times kernel height times kernel width
options.B: The number of pixels in the input image
options.C: The number of input channels
options.D: The number of kernels in the layer

questions.explanation: fan_in must reflect how many terms are summed to produce one output value, which for a convolution is the full spatial and channel extent of one kernel - not the size of the image, since the kernel is shared across positions.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about momentum in gradient descent is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It accumulates a velocity from past gradients rather than using the current gradient alone
options.B: It removes the need to choose a learning rate
options.C: Typical values are close to but below 1, for example 0.9
options.D: It damps oscillations across a narrow ravine while accelerating along its floor

questions.explanation: Momentum changes how the gradient is applied but a learning rate is still required. A momentum of 0.9 averages roughly the last ten gradients.

questions.question: Adam divides its bias-corrected first moment by the square root of its bias-corrected second moment. What does this division achieve?

options.A: It makes the update independent of the loss function
options.B: It removes the need for weight initialisation
options.C: It guarantees the loss decreases monotonically
options.D: It gives each parameter an individually scaled step, larger where gradients are consistently small

questions.explanation: Dividing by the typical gradient magnitude makes the effective step roughly scale-free per parameter, which is why Adam copes well with badly scaled problems.

questions.question: Adam's moment estimates are initialised at zero. Without bias correction, what goes wrong?

options.A: The estimates are biased towards zero in the first iterations, making the early steps too small
options.B: The estimates diverge
options.C: Nothing; bias correction is a numerical convenience only
options.D: The second moment becomes negative

questions.explanation: A running average that starts at zero underestimates the true value until enough terms accumulate. The correction divides by 1 - beta^k, which is largest at the start and tends to 1 later.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the number of weights in a convolutional layer is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It depends on the stride
options.B: It depends on the spatial size of the input image
options.C: It depends on the number of input channels
options.D: It depends on the number of kernels

questions.explanation: Weight sharing is exactly what makes the parameter count independent of the image size, and the stride only changes where the kernel is evaluated, not how many weights it has.

questions.question: An input of width 32 is convolved with a kernel of width 5, stride 1 and no padding. What is the output width?
questions.explanation: With valid padding the output width is (W - k)/s + 1 = (32 - 5)/1 + 1 = 28.

questions.question: An input of width 32 is convolved with a kernel of width 3, stride 2 and padding 1. What is the output width?
questions.explanation: The formula gives (32 - 3 + 2*1)/2 + 1 = 16, so a stride of 2 with same-style padding halves the resolution.

questions.question: A convolutional layer zero-pads the image plane but treats the channel axis differently. How is the channel axis handled?

options.A: The channel axis is pooled before the convolution
options.B: It is also zero padded, so the channel count is preserved
options.C: Each channel is convolved independently and the results are concatenated
options.D: The kernel spans all input channels at once, so the channel axis behaves like a valid convolution and collapses

questions.explanation: Zero padding the image plane keeps the spatial size, while summing over the full channel extent is what turns a multi-channel input into one feature map per kernel.

questions.question: Why does an even-sized convolution kernel require asymmetric padding to preserve the spatial size?

options.A: Because an even kernel has no centre pixel, so the padding cannot be split equally
options.B: Because even kernels have twice as many weights
options.C: It does not; even kernels use the same padding as odd ones
options.D: Because the stride must then be even as well
