questions.explanation: An odd kernel of width k pads (k-1)/2 on both sides. For even k no integer works on both sides, so one side receives one more row or column than the other.

questions.question: A strided convolution is implemented as a stride-1 convolution followed by discarding rows and columns. What must the backward pass do to the incoming error tensor?

options.A: Scale it by the stride
options.B: Insert zeros at the positions that were discarded, restoring the stride-1 shape
options.C: Average it over each stride window
options.D: Transpose it

questions.explanation: Positions that produced no output can have received no gradient, so they contribute zero. Inserting those zeros restores the shape the stride-1 backward pass expects.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about pooling layers is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: They introduce a degree of invariance to small translations
options.B: They have no trainable parameters
options.C: They reduce the spatial dimensions of their input
options.D: They increase the number of channels

questions.explanation: Pooling acts within each channel independently, so the channel count is unchanged. Only a convolutional layer changes the depth.

questions.question: What must a max-pooling layer store during its forward pass in order to backpropagate?

options.A: The positions of the maxima, so the error is routed only to those inputs
options.B: The mean of each pooling window
options.C: Nothing, since pooling has no parameters
options.D: The full input tensor

questions.explanation: Only the winning element influenced the output, so it receives the whole gradient and every other element in the window receives zero.

questions.question: Unlike a convolutional layer, a pooling layer is normally applied without padding. What follows from that?

options.A: The output has the same spatial size as the input
options.B: The layer becomes trainable
options.C: Border positions where the window does not fit entirely are discarded
options.D: The output has more channels than the input

questions.explanation: This is a valid-style operation, so an input whose size is not an exact multiple of the window loses a strip at the border.

questions.question: What role does a flatten layer play between a convolutional stack and a fully connected layer?

options.A: It reduces the number of channels
options.B: It reshapes the multi-dimensional feature maps into one vector per sample
options.C: It normalises the activations
options.D: It averages the feature maps into a single value per channel

questions.explanation: Averaging per channel describes global average pooling, a different operation. Flatten preserves every value and only changes the shape, so its backward pass is the inverse reshape.

questions.question: Which of the following statements comparing L1 and L2 regularization is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: L1 is differentiable everywhere, unlike L2
options.B: L1 drives many weights to exactly zero, producing a sparse solution
options.C: Both add a penalty term to the loss and a corresponding term to the gradient
options.D: L2 shrinks large weights most strongly and rarely produces exact zeros

questions.explanation: It is the other way round: the absolute value has a kink at zero, so L1 needs a sub-gradient there, while the quadratic L2 penalty is smooth everywhere.

questions.question: You train two copies of a network, one with L1 and one with L2 regularization, and plot a histogram of the learned weights. Which observation indicates the L1 model?

options.A: Two peaks at the extreme positive and negative values
options.B: A uniform spread over the whole range
options.C: A large spike exactly at zero with the remaining weights spread around it
options.D: A narrow bell shape centred on zero

questions.explanation: L2's gradient shrinks in proportion to the weight, so weights approach zero without arriving. L1's gradient has constant magnitude, which pushes small weights all the way to zero.

questions.question: Which problem is dropout specifically designed to address?

options.A: Exploding activations in recurrent layers
options.B: Vanishing gradients in deep networks
options.C: Class imbalance in the training set
options.D: Co-adaptation, where units only work in the presence of specific other units

questions.explanation: Because any unit may be absent on any iteration, no unit can depend on a particular partner, so the layer is pushed towards more redundant and independent features.

questions.question: With inverted dropout, the surviving activations are divided by the keep probability during training. What does this buy you?

options.A: Faster convergence, since the activations are larger
options.B: The dropout mask no longer needs to be stored
options.C: The gradient no longer has to pass through the dropout layer
options.D: The expected activation is unchanged, so the test-time forward pass needs no rescaling at all

questions.explanation: Without the correction the expected activation would be scaled by p during training, and inference would have to multiply by p to match. Inverted dropout moves that correction into the training pass instead.

questions.question: Which layers behave differently during training and during inference? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Dropout
options.B: Max pooling
options.C: Batch normalization
options.D: Fully connected

questions.explanation: Dropout is inactive at test time, and batch normalization switches from batch statistics to its stored running estimates. Pooling and fully connected layers compute the same function in both phases.

questions.question: At test time, batch normalization cannot use the statistics of the current batch. What does it use instead, and why?

options.A: The statistics of the first training batch only
options.B: Zero mean and unit variance, since the input is already normalised
options.C: Statistics recomputed on the test set
options.D: A moving average of the mean and variance accumulated during training, so a prediction does not depend on which other samples share the batch

questions.explanation: Using the test batch would make a single sample's prediction depend on its batch-mates, so the same image could be classified differently depending on what it was batched with.

questions.question: In convolutional batch normalization, over which elements are the mean and variance computed?

options.A: Over each sample separately, across channels and positions
options.B: Over the whole tensor, giving a single scalar pair
options.C: Over each pixel position separately, across the batch
options.D: Over the batch and all spatial positions, giving one scalar pair per channel

questions.explanation: This mirrors weight sharing in the convolution itself: a kernel is applied identically at every position, so its output statistics are pooled over all of them.

questions.question: Batch normalization learns a scale gamma and a shift beta, initialised to 1 and 0. Why are those particular starting values chosen?

options.A: They prevent the layer from ever changing the distribution
options.B: They make the layer non-trainable
options.C: They make the layer an identity map of the normalised signal, so it does not disturb training initially
options.D: They guarantee the output has zero mean

questions.explanation: Starting from the identity lets the network recover un-normalised behaviour if that turns out to be better, rather than forcing a transformation on it from the first iteration.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about batch normalization is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It normalizes the weights of each layer
options.B: It smooths the loss landscape, which permits larger learning rates
options.C: It can be skipped entirely at inference time
options.D: It reduces the shift in each layer's input distribution caused by updates to earlier layers

questions.explanation: It normalizes activations, not weights, and it cannot be skipped at inference - the trained network depends on the transformation being applied, using the stored running statistics.

questions.question: What makes a recurrent layer suitable for sequential data?

options.A: It is invariant to the order of the inputs
options.B: It carries a hidden state forward, so the output at one step can depend on earlier inputs
options.C: It processes every time step in parallel
options.D: It requires fewer parameters than a fully connected layer

questions.explanation: Order invariance would defeat the purpose entirely - the hidden state is precisely what makes the layer order dependent.

questions.question: A recurrent cell shares one weight set across all time steps. During backpropagation through time, why must the weights not be updated at each step as you iterate backwards?

options.A: Changing the weights mid-pass changes the function being differentiated, so the remaining gradients no longer match the forward pass that produced them
options.B: The optimizer state would be lost
options.C: The hidden state would be reset
options.D: The learning rate would have to be rescaled by the sequence length

questions.explanation: The correct procedure is to accumulate the gradient contributions from every time step and apply a single update once the backward pass is complete.

questions.question: You build a recurrent cell whose only non-linearity is a ReLU, and all weights and inputs happen to be positive. What behaviour should you expect over a long sequence?

options.A: The hidden state saturates at 1
options.B: The hidden state oscillates
options.C: The hidden state decays to zero
options.D: The hidden state and its gradients grow without bound, since nothing limits the repeated multiplication

questions.explanation: A bounded non-linearity such as tanh prevents this by squashing the state each step - at the cost of making vanishing gradients the dominant risk instead.

questions.question: The output of a recurrent cell is passed straight to a cross-entropy loss without any final non-linearity. What is wrong?

options.A: The loss would always evaluate to zero
options.B: Cross entropy is not differentiable
options.C: Recurrent cells cannot be used for classification
options.D: Cross entropy expects probabilities, so the unbounded output must first be mapped into a valid range by a sigmoid or softmax

questions.explanation: The logarithm in the cross entropy is undefined for non-positive values, so an unbounded pre-activation cannot be fed to it directly.

questions.question: Why is the derivative of tanh and of the sigmoid conveniently expressed in terms of their own output?

options.A: Because sigmoid'(x) = a(1-a) and tanh'(x) = 1 - a^2 with a the activation, so caching the output is enough for the backward pass
options.B: Because their derivative is constant
options.C: Because both are piecewise linear
options.D: Because both functions are their own derivative

questions.explanation: Storing the activation rather than the input saves recomputing the function during the backward pass, which matters when a cell is unrolled over many time steps.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the LSTM compared to a simple recurrent cell is/are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Its cell state is updated additively, which lets gradients survive over longer sequences
options.B: It removes the need for backpropagation through time
options.C: It uses gates to control what is written to, kept in, and read from the cell state
options.D: It has fewer parameters than a simple recurrent cell

questions.explanation: An LSTM has considerably more parameters because of its gates, and it is still trained with backpropagation through time - the gates change how gradients flow, not the training algorithm.

questions.question: You classify solar cell images where a cell may be cracked, inactive, both, or neither. A colleague proposes a softmax output with cross entropy. Is that a good design choice?

options.A: Yes, softmax is the standard choice for image classification
options.B: No, because the images are grayscale
options.C: No, softmax forces the outputs to compete and sum to one, which cannot represent two labels being active at once
options.D: Yes, provided a fifth 'both' class is added

questions.explanation: This is a multi-label problem, so each label needs an independent sigmoid and a binary cross entropy, letting the outputs be high, low, or both independently.

questions.question: Your defect dataset contains far more healthy cells than defective ones. Why is accuracy a poor metric here, and what is commonly used instead?

options.A: Accuracy requires a softmax output
options.B: Accuracy is fine; F1 is only for regression
options.C: Accuracy cannot be computed for multi-label problems
options.D: A model predicting 'healthy' for everything already scores high accuracy, so the F1 score is used to balance precision and recall on the rare class

questions.explanation: With a heavily imbalanced dataset, accuracy is dominated by the majority class and says almost nothing about whether the defects are actually being found.

questions.question: In a PyTorch training loop, why must the gradients be zeroed at the start of every iteration?

options.A: Because the model would remain in evaluation mode
options.B: Because the loss would become negative
options.C: Because the optimizer would otherwise use a stale learning rate
options.D: Because gradients accumulate by default, so without zeroing each step would use the sum of all gradients seen so far

questions.explanation: Accumulation is deliberate - it enables tricks such as simulating a large batch across several passes - but it means the loop must clear the buffers itself.

questions.question: Which of the following should be done before running a validation pass? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Apply the same random augmentations used during training
options.B: Switch the model to evaluation mode, so dropout and batch norm use their inference behaviour
options.C: Disable gradient computation, since no update follows
options.D: Reset the optimizer's internal state

questions.explanation: Augmenting the validation set would make the score depend on random draws, and the optimizer state must persist across epochs or momentum and Adam would restart every time.

questions.question: Early stopping monitors a metric and halts training when it stops improving. Which metric should be monitored?

options.A: The magnitude of the weight updates
options.B: The validation loss, since the point of stopping is to catch the onset of overfitting
options.C: The test loss, since that is the final measure of quality
options.D: The training loss, since it directly reflects optimisation progress

questions.explanation: The training loss usually keeps falling even as generalisation degrades, and touching the test set for stopping decisions would forfeit its role as an unbiased estimate.

questions.question: In a residual block, the input is added to the output of the convolutional path. Why does this help train deep networks?

options.A: It reduces the number of parameters
options.B: It removes the need for a non-linearity
options.C: It gives the gradient a direct path that skips the block, so the effective depth for gradient flow stays small
options.D: It normalises the activations

questions.explanation: It also means a block can represent the identity by driving its residual branch to zero, so adding depth cannot easily make the network worse.

questions.question: Inside a residual block the convolutional path changes both the stride and the number of channels. What must be done to the skip connection?

options.A: The block must be split into two blocks
options.B: It must be transformed, typically by a 1x1 convolution with the matching stride and channel count, so the shapes agree
options.C: It must be replaced by a concatenation
options.D: Nothing, addition broadcasts automatically

questions.explanation: The addition requires identical shapes on both branches, so the shortcut needs a projection whenever the residual path changes the resolution or the depth.

questions.question: A convolutional network ends with global average pooling before its classifier rather than a flatten. What is the practical advantage?

options.A: It increases the number of features available to the classifier
options.B: Each feature map collapses to one value, so the classifier's input size no longer depends on the spatial size of the image
options.C: It makes the network trainable without backpropagation
options.D: It removes the need for an activation function

questions.explanation: A flatten ties the classifier's input dimension to the input resolution, so the network only accepts one image size. Global average pooling removes that constraint and also cuts a large number of parameters.

questions.question: You have a small labelled dataset of defect images and access to a network pre-trained on a large natural-image dataset. What is the standard approach?

options.A: Train from scratch, since the two domains differ
options.B: Use the pre-trained network without any training and accept its original classes
options.C: Reuse the pre-trained weights and fine-tune on the new task, since early features such as edges transfer
options.D: Average the weights of several randomly initialised networks

questions.explanation: Transfer learning is most valuable exactly in this situation: the new dataset is too small to learn good low-level features, but those features are largely task independent.

questions.question: Your grayscale images must be fed to a network whose first convolution expects three input channels. What is the simplest correct fix?

options.A: Repeat the single channel three times to form an RGB-shaped input
options.B: Pad the tensor with two channels of zeros
options.C: Change the loss function
options.D: Feed the image three times in sequence

questions.explanation: Replicating the channel keeps the image's appearance intact for filters that expect natural-image statistics, whereas zero channels would present the network with a strongly coloured input it never saw during pre-training.

questions.question: What is one epoch of training?

options.A: One forward pass through the network
options.B: One evaluation of the validation set
options.C: One complete pass over the training set
options.D: One parameter update

questions.explanation: An epoch consists of many updates - one per mini-batch - so the number of updates per epoch is the dataset size divided by the batch size.

questions.question: Why is data augmentation applied to the training set but not to the validation set?

options.A: Augmentation only works on large datasets
options.B: Augmentation is too slow for validation
options.C: The validation set has no labels
options.D: The validation score should measure model quality, not vary with random transforms

questions.explanation: A validation score that changes on every evaluation cannot be compared across epochs, which would make early stopping and model selection unreliable.

questions.question: A dataset stores labels in a separate file that maps each image filename to a class index. What must a data generator do with them?

options.A: Shuffle them independently of the images
options.B: Convert them to floating point images
options.C: Return them alongside the corresponding images, in the same order as the batch
options.D: Normalise them to zero mean and unit variance

questions.explanation: Shuffling labels independently of the images would break the correspondence entirely, which is a classic source of a model that never learns.

questions.question: Which of these layers has no trainable parameters?

options.A: ReLU
options.B: Batch normalization
options.C: Convolutional
options.D: Fully connected

questions.explanation: ReLU is a fixed element-wise function. Batch normalization does have trainable parameters, its scale and shift, in addition to the statistics it tracks.

questions.question: In which order are layers executed during the forward and backward passes?

options.A: Both passes run from input to loss
options.B: Both passes run from loss to input
options.C: Trainable layers first, then the rest
options.D: Forward from input to loss, backward from loss to input

questions.explanation: The backward pass must reverse the forward order, because each layer's gradient depends on the gradient of the layer that consumed its output.

questions.question: What does a plain stochastic gradient descent update do to a weight?

options.A: Adds the gradient scaled by the learning rate
options.B: Subtracts the gradient scaled by the learning rate
options.C: Replaces it with the gradient
options.D: Multiplies it by the learning rate

questions.explanation: The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase, so minimising the loss means stepping against it.

questions.question: What is the output of a cross-entropy loss for one mini-batch?

options.A: One value per sample
options.B: A single scalar summarising the whole batch
options.C: A matrix the same shape as the predictions
options.D: One value per class

questions.explanation: Gradient descent needs a scalar objective, so the per-sample losses are summed or averaged before the backward pass begins.

questions.question: What is the output of ReLU for the input vector (-2, 0, 3)?
questions.explanation: ReLU passes non-negative values unchanged and clamps everything negative to zero.

questions.question: What does the softmax function guarantee about its output vector?

options.A: All entries are non-negative and sum to one
options.B: The entries are sorted in descending order
options.C: Exactly one entry is 1 and the rest are 0
options.D: All entries lie between -1 and 1

questions.explanation: That is precisely what allows the output to be interpreted as a probability distribution over the classes.

questions.question: Why must the weights of a layer be initialised randomly rather than all to the same value?

options.A: To speed up the forward pass
options.B: To break symmetry, so that units in a layer can learn different features
options.C: To make training reproducible
options.D: To reduce the number of parameters

questions.explanation: Identical weights give identical gradients, so the units would remain copies of one another for the whole of training.

questions.question: What quantity does an optimizer need in addition to the current weights?

options.A: The network architecture
options.B: The full training set
options.C: The validation accuracy
options.D: The gradient of the loss with respect to those weights

questions.explanation: This separation is what allows the same optimizer to be used with any layer type, since it only sees weights and gradients.

questions.question: A pooling layer with a 2x2 window and stride 2 is applied to a 32x32 feature map. What is the output size?
questions.explanation: A stride of 2 halves each spatial dimension, which is the usual way a convolutional stack reduces resolution as depth increases.

questions.question: How many channels does a max-pooling layer produce from an input with 64 channels?
questions.explanation: Pooling operates within each channel independently, so only the spatial dimensions change.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a weight initialisation scheme such as Xavier or He?

options.A: To reduce the number of parameters
options.B: To keep the scale of the activations and gradients stable across layers at the start of training
options.C: To decide how many layers the network needs
options.D: To choose the learning rate automatically

questions.explanation: A poor initial scale makes the signal either vanish or explode as it propagates, so training stalls before it begins.

questions.question: Which optimizer maintains both a first and a second moment estimate of the gradient?

options.A: Adam
options.B: Gradient clipping
options.C: SGD with momentum
options.D: Plain SGD

questions.explanation: Momentum keeps only the first moment. Adam adds the second, which is what gives it a per-parameter step size.

questions.question: What is the effect of 'same' padding with stride 1?

options.A: The output is smaller than the input
options.B: The number of channels is preserved
options.C: The output has the same spatial size as the input
options.D: The kernel size is halved

questions.explanation: Zero padding replaces the border pixels the kernel would otherwise lose, so layers can be stacked without the feature maps shrinking away.

questions.question: What does the regularization weight, often called alpha or lambda, control?

options.A: The batch size
options.B: How strongly the penalty term influences the total loss relative to the data term
options.C: The fraction of units dropped
options.D: The learning rate

questions.explanation: Too large and the model underfits because the penalty dominates; too small and it has no regularizing effect at all.

questions.question: Which statement about dropout is correct?

options.A: It removes units permanently from the network
options.B: It deactivates a random subset of units on each training iteration
options.C: It reduces the learning rate over time
options.D: It normalises the activations of a layer

questions.explanation: A different random subset is drawn each iteration, so over training the network is effectively an ensemble of many thinned sub-networks.

questions.question: What does batch normalization normalise?

options.A: The weights of the layer
options.B: The learning rate
options.C: The gradients
options.D: The activations, using statistics computed over the mini-batch

questions.explanation: It then applies a learned scale and shift, so the network can undo the normalisation if that turns out to be better.

questions.question: What does the hidden state of a recurrent cell represent?

options.A: The gradient at the current step
options.B: The length of the sequence
options.C: A summary of the sequence seen so far, passed to the next time step
options.D: The network's parameters

questions.explanation: It is the only route by which information from earlier inputs reaches later outputs.

questions.question: Which activation functions are typically used inside an LSTM's gates?

options.A: ReLU and softmax
options.B: Leaky ReLU and ELU
options.C: Linear and softmax
options.D: Sigmoid and tanh

questions.explanation: A gate must output a value between 0 and 1 so it can act as a soft switch, which is exactly the range of the sigmoid.

questions.question: What is a residual or skip connection?

options.A: A connection that skips the loss function
options.B: A connection between two different networks
options.C: A connection that removes a layer during training
options.D: A connection that adds a block's input to its output

questions.explanation: It gives the gradient a short path back through the network and lets a block represent the identity when its residual branch is near zero.

questions.question: Why is a model switched to evaluation mode before inference?

options.A: To enable gradient computation
options.B: To free memory
options.C: So that layers such as dropout and batch normalization use their inference behaviour
options.D: To reset the weights

questions.explanation: Forgetting this is a common bug: dropout stays active and the batch statistics keep updating, so predictions become noisy and non-deterministic.

questions.question: What does the F1 score combine?

options.A: Training and validation error
options.B: Accuracy and loss
options.C: True positives and true negatives
options.D: Precision and recall

questions.explanation: It is their harmonic mean, so it is only high when both are high - a model that finds every defect by flagging everything scores poorly.

questions.question: Which output activation suits a multi-label problem where several labels can be active at once?

options.A: No activation at all
options.B: A softmax over all labels
options.C: A single ReLU
options.D: An independent sigmoid per label

questions.explanation: Softmax forces the outputs to compete for a fixed total, which is exactly wrong when two labels can be true simultaneously.

questions.question: What does global average pooling produce from a feature map with C channels?

options.A: A vector of length equal to the number of pixels
options.B: A single scalar
options.C: A vector of length C, one average per channel
options.D: A feature map of half the spatial size

questions.explanation: Because the spatial extent collapses entirely, the following classifier no longer depends on the input resolution.

questions.question: Your training loss decreases steadily but your validation loss starts rising after epoch 12. What does this indicate and what is the standard response?

options.A: The validation set is mislabelled
options.B: Underfitting; train for longer
options.C: Overfitting from epoch 12 onwards; stop early and keep the model from around that point
options.D: The learning rate is too small; increase it

questions.explanation: The divergence between the two curves is the classic overfitting signature, and the epoch where the validation curve turns is exactly what early stopping is designed to catch.

questions.question: You train a network and the loss does not change at all from the first iteration. Which cause is most plausible?

options.A: The validation set is too small
options.B: The batch size is too large
options.C: The learning rate is zero, or the gradients are not reaching the parameters
options.D: The model has too many layers

questions.explanation: A completely flat loss points at a broken update path - a zeroed learning rate, gradients never computed, or an optimizer that was never given the parameters - rather than at a modelling problem.

questions.question: What does a single neuron compute before its activation is applied?

options.A: The distance to the nearest class centre
options.B: A weighted sum of the inputs plus a bias
options.C: The variance of the inputs
options.D: The product of all inputs

questions.explanation: Everything else in a layer is built on this one affine operation.

questions.question: Which of these separate deep learning from classical machine learning? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Features are learned rather than hand-designed
options.B: Much more training data is usually needed
options.C: Representation and classifier are trained together
options.D: No optimisation is involved

questions.explanation: Deep models are still trained by optimisation. What changed is that nobody designs the features by hand.

questions.question: You have 200 labelled images and a set of features that already work well. What should you try first?

options.A: A network trained without validation data
options.B: A deeper network with no regularisation
options.C: A deep network trained from scratch
options.D: A classical model on those features

questions.explanation: 200 samples is far too few to learn a representation. Fine-tuning a pretrained network would be the other sensible option.

questions.question: Your model scores 99% on training data and 62% on validation data. What is happening?

options.A: It is overfitting
options.B: The learning rate is too small
options.C: It is underfitting
options.D: The validation set is too big

questions.explanation: The fix is more data, augmentation or regularisation, not more capacity.

questions.question: What does model capacity refer to?

options.A: The largest usable batch size
options.B: The range of functions it can fit
options.C: The memory the model needs
options.D: The size of the training set

questions.explanation: Capacity grows with width and depth and sits at the heart of the bias-variance trade-off.

questions.question: Why did the XOR result damage early neural network research?

options.A: A limit of one linear layer was read as a limit of the whole idea
options.B: It showed networks can never approximate functions
options.C: It showed convolution was required
options.D: It proved backpropagation was wrong

questions.explanation: One hidden layer solves XOR. The result was correct but got over-generalised.

questions.question: Which of these enabled the deep learning breakthrough around 2012? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Activations that do not saturate
options.B: Large labelled datasets
options.C: The invention of backpropagation
options.D: Training on GPUs

questions.explanation: Backpropagation was decades old by then. Scale and a few training tricks were what changed.

questions.question: What separates supervised from unsupervised learning?

options.A: Only supervised learning uses networks
options.B: Supervised learning trains against given labels
options.C: Unsupervised learning cannot be measured
options.D: Unsupervised learning needs more compute

questions.explanation: Unsupervised objectives still exist, they just come from the data rather than from labels.

questions.question: All your class A photos were shot outdoors and all class B photos indoors. Why is that a problem?

options.A: The images have different sizes
options.B: Outdoor images cannot be normalised
options.C: Lighting separates the classes, so the model can learn that instead
options.D: It is fine if the dataset is large

questions.explanation: The model takes whichever shortcut is easiest, and that shortcut breaks as soon as the correlation does.

questions.question: A model underfits badly. Which change is most likely to help?

options.A: Increase the model capacity
options.B: Shrink the training set
options.C: Add dropout
options.D: Add more weight decay

questions.explanation: Dropout and weight decay both fight overfitting, so they would make underfitting worse.

questions.question: Why is a training, validation and test split used rather than just training and test?

options.A: To make training faster
options.B: So tuning decisions do not contaminate the final score
options.C: Because test sets are usually mislabelled
options.D: To reduce the parameter count

questions.explanation: Once you tune on a split, its score is no longer an honest estimate.

questions.question: What does backpropagation compute?

options.A: The updated weights
options.B: The best learning rate
options.C: Gradients of the loss for every parameter
options.D: The forward activations

questions.explanation: Applying those gradients is the optimizer's job, which is why any optimizer can be swapped in.

questions.question: You stack two fully connected layers with nothing between them. What have you built?

options.A: A model with twice the capacity
options.B: A model that cannot be trained
options.C: Something equivalent to one fully connected layer
options.D: A convolutional layer

questions.explanation: Two affine maps compose into one. Depth only pays off with a non-linearity in between.

questions.question: Which statements about vanishing gradients are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Early layers learn more slowly than late ones
options.B: Long sequences make it more likely
options.C: Saturating activations make it worse
options.D: Dropout is the standard cure

questions.explanation: Dropout targets overfitting. The cures here are better activations, normalisation and skip connections.

questions.question: Why does each layer learn a bias as well as weights?

options.A: It lets the boundary miss the origin
options.B: It normalises the inputs
options.C: It keeps activations positive
options.D: It stops weights growing

questions.explanation: Without a bias every layer is pinned to the origin, which is a real restriction.

questions.question: You set every weight in a layer to the same non-zero value. What happens?

options.A: Gradients explode at once
options.B: The units stay identical and never differentiate
options.C: Training is slower but works
options.D: The biases break the tie

questions.explanation: Identical weights give identical gradients, so the whole layer behaves like one unit.

questions.question: A fully connected layer maps 100 inputs to 50 outputs. How many parameters, biases included?
questions.explanation: 100 x 50 weights plus one bias per output gives 5050.

questions.question: What does the universal approximation theorem actually promise?

options.A: One hidden layer can approximate any continuous function
options.B: Few hidden units are needed
options.C: Deeper is always better
options.D: Gradient descent finds the global optimum

questions.explanation: It is an existence result only. It says nothing about how wide the layer must be or whether training will find it.

questions.question: Given that one hidden layer is enough in theory, why build deep networks?

options.A: Deep networks never overfit
options.B: Shallow networks cannot use ReLU
options.C: Depth reuses features, so fewer units are needed
options.D: Deep networks have convex losses

questions.explanation: The width a shallow network would need is often impractical. Depth expresses the same thing far more compactly.

questions.question: You feed a uniform grey image into a trained classifier. Are all class scores equal?

options.A: Yes, the input has no class information
options.B: Only for a batch size of one
options.C: Yes, if the output is a softmax
options.D: No, each class has different weights

questions.explanation: Equal scores would need identical weight vectors for every class, which training never produces.

questions.question: Your loss turns into NaN partway through training. What is the first thing to suspect?

options.A: Dropout was left on
options.B: The validation set is too large
options.C: The learning rate is too high
options.D: The batch size is too small

questions.explanation: Lower the rate, clip the gradients, and check for a log of zero in the loss.

questions.question: Which quantity does a fully connected layer have to keep from its forward pass?

options.A: Nothing at all
options.B: Its own output
options.C: The learning rate
options.D: Its input

questions.explanation: The weight gradient is built from the stored input and the incoming error.

questions.question: In what order do the forward and backward passes run?

options.A: Both input to loss
options.B: Forward input to loss, backward loss to input
options.C: Trainable layers first
options.D: Both loss to input

questions.explanation: Each layer's gradient depends on the one above it, so the backward pass must reverse the order.

questions.question: Adding more layers to a plain network makes the training error go up. What does that suggest?

options.A: Overfitting
options.B: An optimisation problem, not a generalisation one
options.C: The batch size is too large
options.D: The labels are wrong

questions.explanation: Overfitting would lower the training error. This is the degradation problem that motivated ResNet.

questions.question: How does a gradient descent step change a weight?

options.A: Adds the scaled gradient
options.B: Subtracts the scaled gradient
options.C: Replaces it with the gradient
options.D: Multiplies it by the gradient

questions.explanation: The gradient points uphill, so minimising means stepping against it.

questions.question: What does the learning rate control?

options.A: How often validation runs
options.B: How far each step moves
options.C: How many samples each update uses
options.D: How many layers get updated

questions.explanation: Too large and it overshoots, too small and training crawls.

questions.question: Which statements about SGD versus full-batch gradient descent are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: SGD gradients are noisier
options.B: The noise can help escape poor minima
options.C: Full-batch descent needs shuffling
options.D: SGD makes many more updates per epoch

questions.explanation: Full-batch uses the whole set for each update, so the order it sees the data in makes no difference.

questions.question: Three loss curves are very noisy, mildly noisy and smooth. Which method produced the smooth one?

options.A: Mini-batch with batch size 8
options.B: Single-sample SGD
options.C: Mini-batch with batch size 2
options.D: Full-batch gradient descent

questions.explanation: Averaging over more samples lowers the variance of the gradient estimate.

questions.question: Which loss goes with a softmax output for multi-class problems?

options.A: Mean absolute error
options.B: Cross entropy
options.C: Hinge loss
options.D: Mean squared error

questions.explanation: Cross entropy is the negative log-likelihood of the right class under the softmax.

questions.question: Which loss suits a regression task with roughly Gaussian noise?

options.A: Hinge loss
options.B: Squared error
options.C: Binary cross entropy
options.D: Cross entropy

questions.explanation: Squared error is the maximum likelihood choice under Gaussian noise.

questions.question: Your binary classifier ends with ReLU, then sigmoid, and thresholds at 0.5. Is this sensible?

options.A: Yes, if you raise the threshold
options.B: Yes, the sigmoid bounds the output
options.C: No, nothing can score below 0.5
options.D: No, because sigmoid saturates

questions.explanation: ReLU output is never negative and sigmoid(0) is 0.5, so every sample comes out positive.

questions.question: Why decay the learning rate as training goes on?

options.A: It reduces the parameter count
options.B: It stops gradients vanishing
options.C: A big step cannot settle into a minimum
options.D: Gradients grow over time

questions.explanation: The step that made early progress is too coarse near the end, so the loss just bounces around.

questions.question: Which statements about momentum are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Values near 0.9 are common
options.B: It builds a velocity from past gradients
options.C: It damps oscillation across a ravine
options.D: It replaces the learning rate

questions.explanation: A learning rate is still needed. Momentum changes how the gradient is applied, not whether it is scaled.

questions.question: What does Adam add over plain momentum?

options.A: Weight decay
options.B: A larger learning rate
options.C: A second moment, giving per-parameter step sizes
options.D: Gradient clipping

questions.explanation: Dividing by the typical gradient size makes each parameter's step roughly scale-free.

questions.question: Why does Adam apply bias correction?

options.A: The learning rate is too high early on
options.B: The moment estimates start at zero and read too low
options.C: The gradients can be negative
options.D: The bias terms are otherwise ignored

questions.explanation: A running average starting from zero underestimates until enough terms build up.

questions.question: Would an activation that is flat below -0.5, linear in between, and flat above 0.5 train well?

options.A: No, units in the flat parts get no gradient
options.B: Yes, it is a bounded ReLU
options.C: Yes, bounded outputs help
options.D: No, it is discontinuous

questions.explanation: It is continuous, so that is not the issue. The flat regions kill the learning signal.

questions.question: Training loss sits high and flat from the first epoch. What is worth checking? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Whether the learning rate is far too small
options.B: Whether dropout should be added
options.C: Whether the model has enough capacity
options.D: Whether labels line up with inputs

questions.explanation: A flat high loss means underfitting or a bug. Dropout would only make it worse.

questions.question: You feed a GAN sorted batches: all dogs, then all cats. Why is that bad?

options.A: The discriminator cannot handle two classes
options.B: The batches are too large
options.C: Each batch gradient is biased towards one class
options.D: It is fine within one epoch

questions.explanation: Mini-batch methods assume each batch represents the data, and sorted batches break that.

questions.question: What does a loss function return for one mini-batch?

options.A: A matrix of gradients
options.B: One value per sample
options.C: One value per class
options.D: A single number

questions.explanation: Gradient descent needs a scalar, so per-sample losses are summed or averaged first.

questions.question: Why is ReLU usually preferred to sigmoid in hidden layers?

options.A: It is easier to differentiate
options.B: It has trainable parameters
options.C: Its gradient is 1 on the positive side
options.D: It is bounded, so activations cannot blow up

questions.explanation: The sigmoid derivative peaks at 0.25 and nearly vanishes in its tails, so gradients shrink layer by layer.

questions.question: What problem does leaky ReLU fix?

options.A: Exploding gradients
options.B: Units that stop learning once their input stays negative
options.C: Slow convergence on the positive side
options.D: Overfitting

questions.explanation: A small negative slope keeps a gradient alive so the unit can recover.

questions.question: Why does a convolutional layer need far fewer weights than a fully connected one?

options.A: It only sees part of the image
options.B: It ignores colour channels
options.C: One kernel is reused at every position
options.D: It uses fewer bits per weight

questions.explanation: Weight sharing also encodes the idea that a useful pattern is worth spotting anywhere.

questions.question: Which claim about a valid convolution is false?

options.A: It keeps the input size
options.B: It uses no padding
options.C: It drops partial-overlap positions
options.D: Its output is smaller than same padding gives

questions.explanation: That describes same padding. Valid convolution shrinks each dimension by k-1.

questions.question: What is the receptive field of a unit after two stacked 3x3 convolutions, stride 1?
questions.explanation: Each layer widens it by 2. Two 3x3 layers beat one 5x5 on parameter count and add a non-linearity.

questions.question: What is a 1x1 convolution good for?

options.A: Raising the spatial resolution
options.B: Widening the receptive field
options.C: Replacing the activation
options.D: Mixing channels and changing their number cheaply

questions.explanation: It is a per-pixel linear map across channels, often used to cut depth before an expensive layer.

questions.question: What does a dilated convolution buy you?

options.A: Faster training
options.B: A wider receptive field at no extra parameter cost
options.C: Fewer channels
options.D: Guaranteed translation invariance

questions.explanation: Gaps between the kernel taps widen the field fast with depth, which is why segmentation models like it.

questions.question: Which statements about pooling are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It has no trainable parameters
options.B: It shrinks the spatial dimensions
options.C: It adds some translation tolerance
options.D: It increases the channel count

questions.explanation: Pooling works inside each channel, so the depth is untouched.

questions.question: What does max pooling have to remember for its backward pass?

options.A: The whole input
options.B: The window averages
options.C: Nothing, it has no parameters
options.D: Where the maxima were

questions.explanation: Only the winning element affected the output, so only it receives gradient.

questions.question: What does a flatten layer do between convolutions and a dense layer?

options.A: Reshapes the feature maps into a vector
options.B: Cuts the channel count
options.C: Normalises the activations
options.D: Averages each channel to one value

questions.explanation: Averaging per channel is global average pooling, a different thing. Flatten keeps every value.

questions.question: Why is a stride above 1 sometimes called subsampling?

options.A: It averages nearby pixels
options.B: It cuts the channel count
options.C: It samples positions at random
options.D: It only evaluates the kernel every s positions

questions.explanation: That is why a strided convolution can be done as stride 1 followed by dropping rows and columns.

questions.question: Why does an even kernel need uneven padding to keep the input size?

options.A: The stride must also be even
options.B: It has no centre pixel to balance around
options.C: It does not, padding is the same
options.D: It has twice the weights

questions.explanation: An odd kernel pads (k-1)/2 on each side. For an even one no integer works on both sides.

questions.question: What is regularisation for?

options.A: Shrinking the model file
options.B: Improving generalisation by limiting complexity
options.C: Speeding up convergence
options.D: Removing the validation set

questions.explanation: It trades a little training accuracy for a smaller gap to unseen data.

questions.question: Which of these are regularisation techniques? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Data augmentation
options.B: Raising the learning rate
options.C: Weight decay
options.D: Dropout

questions.explanation: A larger learning rate just destabilises training.

questions.question: Which statements comparing L1 and L2 are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: L1 is smooth everywhere
options.B: Both add a term to the loss
options.C: L1 pushes many weights to exactly zero
options.D: L2 shrinks large weights hardest

questions.explanation: It is L2 that is smooth. The absolute value has a kink at zero.

questions.question: A weight histogram shows a tall spike exactly at zero. Which regulariser was used?

options.A: None
options.B: L2
options.C: L1
options.D: Dropout

questions.explanation: L2 shrinks weights towards zero without reaching it. L1 has constant gradient, so it arrives.

questions.question: Your model overfits and you suspect many input features are useless. Which regulariser first?

options.A: Removing the biases
options.B: L2, since it shrinks everything
options.C: L1, since it zeroes irrelevant weights
options.D: A bigger batch size

questions.explanation: L2 would shrink those weights but leave them contributing noise.

questions.question: What is dropout actually attacking?

options.A: Class imbalance
options.B: Exploding activations
options.C: Vanishing gradients
options.D: Units that only work alongside particular partners

questions.explanation: If any partner might vanish, the layer has to learn more redundant features.

questions.question: What does dropout do at inference time?

options.A: Drops the same fraction as in training
options.B: Removes itself from the model
options.C: Drops units but doubles the outputs
options.D: Nothing, the full network is used

questions.explanation: Predictions need to be deterministic, which is why the scaling correction happens during training.

questions.question: Inverted dropout divides surviving activations by the keep probability during training. Why?

options.A: It lets gradients bypass the layer during backpropagation
options.B: It keeps the expected activation the same, so testing needs no rescaling
options.C: It makes the activations larger, so training converges sooner
options.D: It removes the need to store the mask used in the forward pass

questions.explanation: The correction has to happen somewhere. Doing it in training leaves inference clean.

questions.question: Why can batch normalisation not use batch statistics at test time?

options.A: A prediction would depend on the other samples in the batch
options.B: The statistics are undefined
options.C: Batches are too small then
options.D: It would be too slow

questions.explanation: The same image could get different answers depending on what it was batched with, so stored running estimates are used.

questions.question: In convolutional batch normalisation, what are the statistics computed over?

options.A: The batch and all positions, one pair per channel
options.B: Each sample separately
options.C: Each pixel position separately
options.D: The whole tensor at once

questions.explanation: It mirrors weight sharing: one kernel applies everywhere, so its outputs are pooled everywhere.

questions.question: Batch normalisation starts with scale 1 and shift 0. Why those values?

options.A: They lock the distribution
options.B: They force zero mean output
options.C: The layer starts as an identity on the normalised signal
options.D: They make it non-trainable

questions.explanation: Starting from the identity means the layer does not disturb training before it has learned anything.

questions.question: Which statements about batch normalisation are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It reduces drift in layer inputs
options.B: It smooths the loss surface
options.C: It normalises the weights
options.D: It can be skipped at inference

questions.explanation: It normalises activations, and skipping it at inference would break the trained network.

questions.question: Why is batch normalisation said to regularise a little?

options.A: It removes parameters
options.B: It lowers the learning rate
options.C: Batch statistics inject noise into each sample
options.D: It adds a penalty to the loss

questions.explanation: The effect fades as batches grow and the statistics settle.

questions.question: Why does data augmentation act as a regulariser?

options.A: It widens the training distribution, so memorising gets harder
options.B: It rescales the activations of each layer to zero mean
options.C: It reduces how many parameters the network has to fit
options.D: It lowers the effective learning rate as training proceeds

questions.explanation: It also builds in invariances you already know the task has.

questions.question: What is early stopping?

options.A: Stopping when the training loss flattens
options.B: Stopping when validation stops improving, keeping the best model
options.C: Cutting the learning rate when progress slows
options.D: Dropping layers during training

questions.explanation: It limits how far optimisation drifts from the starting point, which is a form of regularisation.

questions.question: What causes internal covariate shift?

options.A: A learning rate that is too small
options.B: Updates to earlier layers changing what later layers see
options.C: Test data drawn from another population
options.D: A non-convex loss

questions.explanation: The second option is dataset shift, a different problem entirely.

questions.question: What does the regularisation weight control?

options.A: The dropout fraction
options.B: The learning rate
options.C: How much the penalty counts against the data term
options.D: The batch size

questions.explanation: Too large and the model underfits, too small and it does nothing.

questions.question: Why do recurrent networks suit time series?

options.A: Their state carries information between steps
options.B: They need fewer parameters
options.C: They ignore input order
options.D: They process all steps at once

questions.explanation: Ignoring order would defeat the point. The recurrence is exactly what makes them order-dependent.

questions.question: Image captioning takes one image and returns a sentence. What mapping is that?

options.A: One to many
options.B: Many to many
options.C: One to one
options.D: Many to one

questions.explanation: Sentiment scoring of a whole review is the mirror image, many to one.

questions.question: Your recurrent cell uses only ReLU and all weights and inputs are positive. What happens over a long sequence?

options.A: The state settles at 1
options.B: The state oscillates
options.C: The state decays to zero
options.D: The state and gradients grow without limit

questions.explanation: A bounded activation like tanh prevents this, at the cost of making vanishing gradients the bigger risk.

questions.question: Why must weights not be updated step by step while backpropagating through time?

options.A: The learning rate would need rescaling
options.B: The hidden state would reset
options.C: Optimizer state would be lost
options.D: The function would change while it is being differentiated

questions.explanation: Gradients are accumulated across all steps and applied once at the end.

questions.question: Which statements about backpropagation through time are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: The network is unrolled over the sequence
options.B: Each step has its own weights
options.C: Truncating it bounds memory use
options.D: Gradients are summed across time steps

questions.explanation: Sharing weights across time is what makes a network recurrent in the first place.

questions.question: What does gradient clipping fix in a recurrent network?

options.A: Exploding gradients
options.B: Overfitting
options.C: Vanishing gradients
options.D: Class imbalance

questions.explanation: Vanishing gradients need architectural fixes such as gating, not clipping.

questions.question: What is the main thing an LSTM buys you over a simple recurrent cell?

options.A: No need for backpropagation through time
options.B: A gated cell state that carries gradients further
options.C: Faster inference
options.D: Fewer parameters

questions.explanation: The cell state is updated additively, so gradients are not squashed at every step.

questions.question: How does a GRU differ from an LSTM?

options.A: It cannot model long dependencies
options.B: It adds a third gate
options.C: It merges gates and drops the separate cell state
options.D: It needs no training

questions.explanation: Fewer parameters and slightly faster training, with accuracy usually close enough that the choice is empirical.

questions.question: What does a bidirectional recurrent network add?

options.A: Immunity to vanishing gradients
options.B: Half the parameters
options.C: A reverse pass, so each step sees future context
options.D: Support for variable lengths

questions.explanation: It only works when the whole sequence is available up front, so it rules out live generation.

questions.question: What is the encoder's job in a sequence-to-sequence model?

options.A: Turning the input into a representation for the decoder
options.B: Shuffling the data
options.C: Generating the output tokens
options.D: Computing the loss

questions.explanation: The split is what lets input and output have different lengths.

questions.question: Why was attention added to encoder-decoder models?

options.A: To remove the encoder
options.B: One fixed context vector is a bottleneck for long inputs
options.C: To cut the parameter count
options.D: To parallelise training

questions.explanation: Parallel training came later with transformers. Attention was first bolted onto recurrent models.

questions.question: What is teacher forcing?

options.A: Raising the learning rate on the last layer of the decoder
options.B: Averaging the predictions of two separately trained models
options.C: Feeding the true previous token instead of the model's own guess
options.D: Freezing the encoder so only the decoder keeps training

questions.explanation: It speeds up early training but leaves a mismatch with inference, where only the model's own output exists.

questions.question: A recurrent output goes straight into cross entropy with no final activation. What is wrong?

options.A: Cross entropy is not differentiable
options.B: Recurrent cells cannot classify
options.C: It expects probabilities, not unbounded values
options.D: The loss would be zero

questions.explanation: The log in the loss is undefined for non-positive inputs.

questions.question: What does an autoencoder learn?

options.A: A map from inputs to labels
options.B: A compact code the input can be rebuilt from
options.C: A distribution over labels
options.D: The best learning rate

questions.explanation: The bottleneck forces it to keep only what reconstruction needs.

questions.question: You build an autoencoder whose code is larger than its input, with no other constraint. What happens?

options.A: It learns a richer representation
options.B: It cannot be trained
options.C: It can just copy the input and learn nothing
options.D: It becomes equivalent to PCA

questions.explanation: An overcomplete autoencoder needs sparsity, noise or a penalty to force it to find structure.

questions.question: What makes a denoising autoencoder different?

options.A: It has no decoder
options.B: It rebuilds clean input from a corrupted copy
options.C: It uses labels
options.D: It cannot use convolutions

questions.explanation: Copying no longer works, so the model has to learn the structure of the data.

questions.question: What does a variational autoencoder encode each input to?

options.A: A single point in latent space
options.B: A binary code
options.C: A class label
options.D: A distribution over latent space

questions.explanation: Its loss pairs reconstruction with a term pulling those distributions towards a prior, which makes sampling possible.

questions.question: Why does a VAE need the reparameterisation trick?

options.A: Sampling blocks gradients, so the randomness is moved aside
options.B: To make the decoder deterministic
options.C: To normalise the latent space
options.D: To cut parameters

questions.explanation: Writing the sample as mean plus sigma times noise leaves a differentiable path back to the encoder.

questions.question: What are the two networks in a GAN?

options.A: Teacher and student
options.B: Generator and discriminator
options.C: Actor and critic
options.D: Encoder and decoder

questions.explanation: The generator makes samples from noise and the discriminator tries to spot them.

questions.question: What is mode collapse?

options.A: The loss goes negative
options.B: The discriminator becomes perfect
options.C: The weights go to zero
options.D: The generator only produces a narrow set of outputs

questions.explanation: A few samples reliably fool the discriminator, so the generator never explores further.

questions.question: How does a conditional GAN let you ask for a specific kind of sample?

options.A: By filtering afterwards
options.B: By feeding both networks a class input
options.C: By retraining for each request
options.D: By enlarging the noise vector

questions.explanation: The training data has to be labelled so the discriminator can judge realism given the class.

questions.question: What is PCA for?

options.A: Penalising large weights so a network generalises better
options.B: Grouping data points into a fixed number of labelled classes
options.C: Finding directions of greatest variance to reduce dimensions
options.D: Generating new samples that resemble the training data

questions.explanation: The components are the leading eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

questions.question: What does k-means do on each iteration?

options.A: Computes the eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix
options.B: Trains a small network to predict the cluster of each point
options.C: Assigns the true class label to every point in the set
options.D: Assigns points to the nearest centre, then moves the centres

questions.explanation: It reaches a local optimum that depends on where the centres started.

questions.question: What can DBSCAN do that k-means cannot?

options.A: Work without a distance measure
options.B: Guarantee a global optimum
options.C: Find non-spherical clusters without being told how many
options.D: Run faster on every dataset

questions.explanation: The trade is that you now have to pick a density threshold and a radius instead.

questions.question: Why are learned word embeddings better than one-hot vectors?

options.A: They prevent overfitting
options.B: They need no data
options.C: They use less memory only
options.D: Similar words end up close together

questions.explanation: One-hot vectors sit at equal distance from each other, so they carry no similarity information.

questions.question: Which of these objectives are unsupervised? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Rebuilding the input through a bottleneck
options.B: Grouping samples by similarity
options.C: Matching a generated distribution to the data
options.D: Cross entropy against human labels

questions.explanation: The last one is the definition of supervised learning.

questions.question: In an encoder-decoder diagram, what does the narrow middle represent?

options.A: The output layer
options.B: The batch dimension
options.C: The loss function
options.D: The bottleneck, or latent code

questions.explanation: Its size decides how much the model is allowed to keep.

questions.question: Why report the final score on a test set rather than the validation set?

options.A: Validation sets have no labels
options.B: Tuning on validation makes its score optimistic
options.C: Validation sets are too small
options.D: Test sets are easier

questions.explanation: Every decision made by looking at a split leaks a little information about it.

questions.question: What does k-fold cross-validation give you?

options.A: Immunity to overfitting
options.B: More training data
options.C: Faster training
options.D: A steadier estimate by averaging over splits

questions.explanation: Most useful on small datasets, where one split can be unlucky. The cost is training k times.

questions.question: When would you use over- or undersampling?

options.A: To evaluate segmentation
options.B: To generate synthetic data
options.C: To handle class imbalance
options.D: To tune dropout

questions.explanation: Weighting the loss by inverse class frequency does much the same without touching the data.

questions.question: Which metric suits a heavily imbalanced binary problem?

options.A: Parameter count
options.B: F1 or area under the ROC curve
options.C: Training loss
options.D: Accuracy

questions.explanation: Predicting the majority class for everything can score above 95% accuracy while detecting nothing.

questions.question: Which statements about transfer learning are true? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Both tasks need the same labels
options.B: It helps most when the new dataset is small
options.C: The final layer usually gets replaced
options.D: Early layers learn generic features that transfer

questions.explanation: Identical labels would make the whole exercise pointless.

questions.question: Why is random search often better than grid search over several hyperparameters?

options.A: It can be run without holding out any validation data
options.B: It always reaches a good setting in fewer total trials
options.C: It is guaranteed to land on the global optimum eventually
options.D: Grid search wastes trials repeating values of the parameter that matters

questions.explanation: Random search samples a fresh value on every axis, so it explores the important one far better.

questions.question: Your network was trained on 32x32 images and ends in flatten plus dense layers. How do you let it take any size?

options.A: Train a separate copy of the network at each resolution
options.B: Add a recurrent layer that reads the feature map row by row
options.C: Swap the dense layers for convolutions and global average pooling
options.D: Resize every incoming image down to 32x32 before the network

questions.explanation: Convolutions have no fixed input size and global pooling collapses whatever is left.

questions.question: Why normalise input features?

options.A: It reduces the number of parameters the model has to learn
options.B: Mismatched scales make the loss surface awkward to descend
options.C: It removes outliers that would otherwise dominate the loss
options.D: It increases the range of functions the model can fit

questions.explanation: A stretched loss surface makes gradient descent zig-zag and forces a smaller learning rate.

questions.question: Which statistics should normalise the test set?

options.A: Those of each sample separately
options.B: Fixed values of 0 and 1
options.C: Those computed on the training set
options.D: Those computed on the test set

questions.explanation: Using test statistics would apply a different transform than the model was trained under.

questions.question: Why switch a model to evaluation mode before inference?

options.A: To free memory
options.B: To reset the weights
options.C: To turn on gradients
options.D: So dropout and batch norm behave correctly

questions.explanation: Forget it and dropout stays active, making predictions noisy and irreproducible.

questions.question: Your training loss falls while validation loss climbs from epoch 12. What do you do?

options.A: Raise the learning rate
options.B: Train longer
options.C: Enlarge the validation set
options.D: Stop around epoch 12 and keep that model

questions.explanation: The point where the two curves part is exactly what early stopping looks for.

questions.question: The loss does not move at all from the first iteration. What is the likely cause?

options.A: The validation set is small
options.B: There are too many layers
options.C: The updates are not reaching the parameters
options.D: The batch size is too large

questions.explanation: A perfectly flat loss usually means a zero learning rate or an optimizer that never got the parameters.

questions.question: What was ResNet's key idea?

options.A: Parallel paths with different kernel sizes
options.B: Learning a residual on top of the identity
options.C: Dropping pooling entirely
options.D: Replacing convolution with attention

questions.explanation: That is what made networks over a hundred layers deep trainable.

questions.question: What is VGG known for?

options.A: Dropping dense layers
options.B: Introducing skip connections
options.C: Introducing attention
options.D: Stacking small 3x3 convolutions instead of large kernels

questions.explanation: Its uniform design made it a popular backbone, though its dense layers are parameter heavy.

questions.question: What does an Inception module do?

options.A: Adds recurrence
options.B: Runs several kernel sizes side by side and concatenates
options.C: Removes all pooling
options.D: Applies dropout everywhere

questions.explanation: 1x1 convolutions cut the depth first so the wider kernels stay affordable.

questions.question: Inside a residual block the stride and channel count change. What happens to the shortcut?

options.A: The block splits in two
options.B: It becomes a concatenation
options.C: It gets a 1x1 convolution to match shapes
options.D: Nothing, addition broadcasts

questions.explanation: Addition needs matching shapes on both branches.

questions.question: What is the core mechanism of a transformer?

options.A: Self-attention over all positions at once
options.B: Clustering of tokens
options.C: Recurrence
options.D: Dilated convolution

questions.explanation: Constant path length between positions avoids the long-range decay that limits recurrent models.

questions.question: How does a Vision Transformer feed an image to the model?

options.A: The whole image as one token
options.B: One token per fixed-size patch
options.C: One token per pixel
options.D: One token per channel

questions.explanation: Attention cost grows quadratically with length, so a token per pixel would be far too expensive.

questions.question: What does global average pooling produce from C feature maps?

options.A: A map at half the size
options.B: A single number
options.C: One value per pixel
options.D: A vector of length C

questions.explanation: Because the spatial extent disappears, the classifier stops depending on input resolution.

questions.question: Two stacked 3x3 convolutions replace one 5x5. What is gained?

options.A: Fewer parameters and an extra non-linearity
options.B: Faster inference only
options.C: Fewer channels
options.D: A wider receptive field

questions.explanation: Both cover 5x5, but the pair uses 18 weights per channel pair instead of 25.

questions.question: What does a saliency map show?

options.A: Which pixels the prediction is most sensitive to
options.B: The label distribution
options.C: The first layer's kernels
options.D: The loss over time

questions.explanation: A quick way to check the model is looking at the object and not the background.

questions.question: What is Grad-CAM for?

options.A: Augmenting data
options.B: A class-specific heat map from a convolutional layer
options.C: Speeding up training
options.D: Compressing a model

questions.explanation: It weights feature maps by their gradients, so the result is coarse but tied to one class.

questions.question: Deeper kernels are hard to read directly. What do people look at instead?

options.A: The learning rate
options.B: The batch statistics
options.C: The activations for a given input
options.D: The bias values

questions.explanation: Activations show what the layer responds to on a real image.

questions.question: In self-attention, where do query, key and value come from?

options.A: A fixed lookup table
options.B: Three learned projections of the same input
options.C: The labels
options.D: Three separately trained networks

questions.explanation: Attending to projections of the same sequence is what makes it self-attention.

questions.question: Why do transformers need positional encoding?

options.A: To prevent overfitting
options.B: Attention alone would treat a shuffled sequence the same
options.C: To normalise attention weights
options.D: To cut parameters

questions.explanation: Recurrent and convolutional models get position from their structure. Attention does not.

questions.question: What does multi-head attention add over one head?

options.A: Freedom from positional encoding
options.B: Faster convergence only
options.C: Several attention patterns in different subspaces
options.D: Guaranteed sparse weights

questions.explanation: One head might track nearby words, another long-range links, which a single averaged head would blur.

questions.question: In an attention diagram, what does a thicker line between two tokens mean?

options.A: A longer distance
options.B: A larger attention weight
options.C: A shared embedding
options.D: A gradient path

questions.explanation: Line thickness or opacity is the usual way to draw how much one token attends to another.

questions.question: What does Q-learning estimate?

options.A: The reward function
options.B: The transition probabilities
options.C: The expected return of an action in a state
options.D: The probability of each action

questions.explanation: The policy follows by taking the argmax, so no model of the environment is needed.

questions.question: What is the difference between exploration and exploitation?

options.A: Exploration maximises immediate reward
options.B: Exploitation is for training, exploration for testing
options.C: Exploitation takes the best known action, exploration tries others
options.D: They mean the same thing

questions.explanation: A purely exploitative agent can settle on a decent action and never find a better one.

questions.question: What does epsilon-greedy do?

options.A: Picks randomly with probability epsilon, greedily otherwise
options.B: Always picks the best-valued action
options.C: Picks in proportion to value
options.D: Explores only in episode one

questions.explanation: Epsilon is usually annealed so the agent explores early and exploits later.

questions.question: Why does DQN keep a replay buffer?

options.A: To make the reward signal differentiable end to end
options.B: To remove the need for a separate target network
options.C: Consecutive transitions are correlated, and sampling breaks that up
options.D: To cut the memory the agent needs while it is learning

questions.explanation: It also lets each transition be reused, which matters when interaction is expensive.

questions.question: What is the target network in DQN for?

options.A: Holding the bootstrap target still between updates
options.B: Computing rewards
options.C: Replacing the replay buffer
options.D: Choosing actions

questions.explanation: Bootstrapping from a network that changes every step means chasing a moving target.

questions.question: What does the discount factor do?

options.A: Weights near rewards above distant ones and keeps the return finite
options.B: Rescales every reward into the range 0 to 1 before use
options.C: Sets how large a step each value update takes
options.D: Decides how often the agent picks a random action

questions.explanation: Close to 1 makes the agent far-sighted, small values make it short-sighted.

questions.question: Which claim about reinforcement learning is wrong?

options.A: A good policy maximises expected future return
options.B: Bellman equations are solvable exactly for small problems
options.C: Temporal difference learning needs the environment dynamics
options.D: Q-learning is off-policy

questions.explanation: Temporal difference learning is model-free and bootstraps from sampled transitions.

questions.question: What do actor-critic methods combine?

options.A: A policy that acts with a value function that judges
options.B: Two environments
options.C: Two value functions
options.D: Supervised and unsupervised learning

questions.explanation: The critic gives the actor a lower-variance signal than the raw return would.

questions.question: What does semantic segmentation produce?

options.A: One label for the image
options.B: A box around each object
options.C: A class label for every pixel
options.D: A separate mask per object

questions.explanation: It does not tell two objects of the same class apart. That is instance segmentation.

questions.question: How does instance segmentation differ from semantic segmentation?

options.A: It separates individual objects of the same class
options.B: It only outputs boxes
options.C: It works only on grayscale
options.D: It uses no convolutions

questions.explanation: Two touching cars become one region under semantic segmentation but two masks under instance.

questions.question: You need to count people in a room. Which task fits?

options.A: Image classification
options.B: Denoising
options.C: Semantic segmentation
options.D: Object detection

questions.explanation: Segmentation merges everyone into one region, so overlapping people cannot be counted.

questions.question: What defines U-Net?

options.A: It has no downsampling
options.B: Skip connections from the encoder to the decoder
options.C: Only dense layers
options.D: No training needed

questions.explanation: Without them the decoder knows roughly where an object is but not where its edge is.

questions.question: What does a fully convolutional network replace to give a dense output?

options.A: The loss
options.B: The dense layers
options.C: The activations
options.D: The pooling layers

questions.explanation: Dense layers fix the input size and throw away spatial layout.

questions.question: Why is classifying each pixel separately with dense layers a poor plan? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It cannot handle more than two classes
options.B: The parameter count explodes
options.C: It loses weight sharing across positions
options.D: It throws away context around the pixel

questions.explanation: The class count is not the obstacle. The lost context and sharing are.

questions.question: Can a segmentation mask be turned into a bounding box?

options.A: Only for convex shapes
options.B: Yes, by taking the extent of the region
options.C: No, they are incompatible
options.D: Only if it is binary

questions.explanation: It works one way only, which is why masks are the more expensive annotation.

questions.question: What does panoptic segmentation combine?

options.A: The training and inference passes into a single network
options.B: Two separate architectures whose outputs are averaged
options.C: A classification head with a regression head for box corners
options.D: Semantic labels for regions with instance masks for objects

questions.explanation: Sky and road are handled semantically, cars and people get their own masks.
